proposals.title: Iterative Knowledge Refinement: A Multi-Pass Framework for Robust Open Knowledge Network Construction
proposals.motivation: Current Open Knowledge Network (OKN) construction and scientific information extraction systems, such as those built on the CORD-19 dataset, predominantly operate in a single pass. This 'single-pass assumption' limits their ability to resolve contradictions, consolidate fragmented facts, and leverage newly discovered relationships to improve earlier extractions. An iterative, multi-pass approach is necessary to build more coherent, accurate, and comprehensive knowledge graphs from large-scale scientific corpora.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative, multi-pass extraction and inference system will produce a more consistent, complete, and higher-fidelity knowledge graph from a scientific corpus than a state-of-the-art single-pass system, as measured by precision, recall, and graph connectivity metrics.
proposals.method: We propose a system built on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, implementing a closed-loop, multi-architecture pipeline. First, a high-recall language model (e.g., a fine-tuned T5 or BERT) performs initial entity and relation extraction from a corpus like CORD-19. The resulting noisy extractions are modeled as a preliminary knowledge graph. In subsequent passes, a Graph Neural Network (GNN) will operate on this graph to identify inconsistencies and infer missing links. The GNN's predictions will generate soft labels to re-train the initial extractor in a bootstrapping manner, while a rule-based consolidator will resolve conflicts. This extractor-GNN-consolidator cycle will iterate until a convergence metric is met.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, generalizable framework for iterative knowledge graph construction from text, 2) A new benchmark and evaluation protocol for multi-pass extraction systems, and 3) An open, iteratively refined knowledge graph derived from a public scientific corpus (e.g., CORD-19) to catalyze further research in automated science.
proposals.novelty: While bootstrapping and self-training are known, applying them in a tightly coupled, graph-aware loop between a neural extractor and a GNN-based reasoner for holistic knowledge graph refinement is a novel synthesis. This challenges the entrenched pipeline paradigm where extraction, integration, and reasoning are sequential, non-communicating stages.

proposals.title: Globally Consistent Confidence: Modeling Interdependent Extractions for Open Knowledge Network Construction
proposals.motivation: Current Open Knowledge Network (OKN) construction from scientific text, such as the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), treats individual relation extractions as independent events. This ignores the rich network of logical and semantic consistency inherent in a corpus, leading to confidence scores that are locally accurate but globally contradictory, thereby polluting the knowledge graph with unreliable edges. This problem directly impedes the quality and trustworthiness of large-scale, automated scientific knowledge synthesis, a core goal of open science infrastructure like Semantic Scholar.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling extraction confidence as interdependent, using a global consistency model across the entire corpus, will significantly improve the precision of the constructed knowledge graph compared to independent, locally-scored extractions.
proposals.method: We will develop a joint inference framework over extractions from a pre-trained language model (e.g., SciBERT). We will construct a factor graph where nodes represent candidate relation tuples (e.g., (Drug X, inhibits, Protein Y)) and edges encode consistency constraints derived from entity linking and ontological rules (e.g., mutual exclusivity, transitivity). Using loopy belief propagation or a neural graph network, we will perform collective inference to update the confidence scores of all extractions simultaneously, promoting globally consistent sets. The system will be evaluated on a curated subset of the CORD-19 corpus with human-annotated relations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable method for improving the fidelity of automated knowledge extraction by enforcing global corpus-level constraints. It will advance the field of OKN development by providing a principled probabilistic framework to resolve extraction conflicts, leading to cleaner, more reliable knowledge graphs for scientific discovery. The resulting software and benchmark will be released as part of the Semantic Scholar open data platform.
proposals.novelty: While joint inference is known in narrow NLP tasks, its application to globally score and reconcile millions of heterogeneous scientific extractions at the scale of an OKN is novel. This moves beyond the standard pipeline of independent extraction and post-hoc filtering, integrating consistency as a first-class citizen in the knowledge construction process.

proposals.title: Precision and Recall in Open Knowledge Networks: A Hybrid Graph-Language Model for Scientific Relation Extraction
proposals.motivation: The construction of large-scale Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) from scientific literature, such as those built on the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), is hampered by the trade-off between extraction precision and recall. Low precision introduces noise that undermines trust in automated scientific insights, while low recall misses critical relationships, limiting the comprehensiveness of the knowledge graph. This directly impacts the utility of platforms like Semantic Scholar for accelerating scientific discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid architecture that integrates a pre-trained language model's contextual understanding with explicit, structured graph constraints from an evolving knowledge base can jointly optimize for both precision and recall in scientific relation extraction, outperforming purely text-based or purely graph-based methods.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, iterative framework. First, a fine-tuned transformer model (e.g., SciBERT) will perform initial extraction from text, generating candidate relational tuples with confidence scores. Second, these candidates will be integrated into a temporal graph representation, where a Graph Neural Network (GNN) will analyze local subgraph structure and consistency to re-score and validate tuples. High-confidence validated tuples will be fed back to fine-tune the language model in the next iteration, creating a self-improving loop. We will evaluate on the CORD-19 corpus and existing biomedical knowledge bases.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable method for building higher-quality scientific OKNs, demonstrated through a validated extraction pipeline and a benchmark dataset. It will advance the technical state-of-the-art in information extraction by formally integrating deep language and graph reasoning, providing a model for continuous knowledge base refinement.
proposals.novelty: While language models and graph methods have been used separately, their tight, iterative integration for mutual refinement in an open-domain scientific extraction task is novel. The proposed feedback loop, where the graph actively teaches the extractor, moves beyond static ensemble approaches.

proposals.title: Topic-Modeled Knowledge Networks: Discovering Latent Relational Domains in a Scientific Tuple Corpus
proposals.motivation: Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) built from scientific text, such as the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), provide structured relational tuples (subject-predicate-object). However, these tuples exist in isolation without a higher-order understanding of the thematic contexts or domains in which certain relations are prevalent. This limits the ability to organize, navigate, and reason over large-scale knowledge graphs effectively, hindering rapid scientific discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying hierarchical topic modeling to the textual context surrounding extracted relational tuples will reveal latent, semantically coherent relational domains, and these domains can be used to structure and enrich a knowledge graph, improving its utility for exploration and downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform to obtain a large corpus of scientific text and pre-extracted tuples (e.g., from S2ORC or CORD-19). For each tuple, we will aggregate the source sentences and surrounding abstract text. We will apply Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and its hierarchical variant (hLDA) to this corpus of tuple-context documents. The resulting topic distributions will be used to annotate tuples and their predicate types with probabilistic domain labels, enabling the construction of a topic-augmented subgraph or a faceted navigation layer over the existing OKN infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for moving from flat knowledge extraction to thematically organized knowledge structures. It will produce a publicly available, topic-annotated scientific tuple corpus and demonstrate its value through a prototype interface for domain-centric exploration of scientific relationships, advancing the goals of rapid OKN development and open science.
proposals.novelty: While topic modeling is well-established for documents and relation extraction is mature, their integration to discover *relational* topics from the context of extracted facts, and using this to impose a thematic structure on a knowledge graph, is a novel synthesis for knowledge network curation and exploration.

proposals.title: Active Bootstrapping for Rapid Scientific Claim Extraction: An Open Knowledge Network Approach
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for rapid, automated synthesis of scientific literature, as seen in efforts like the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19). A core bottleneck in building systems to extract claims or relationships from papers is the creation of high-quality labeled data. Manual annotation of full texts is prohibitively slow and expensive, hindering the development of responsive Open Knowledge Networks for science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning framework, integrated with a pre-trained language model, can efficiently bootstrap a high-performance classifier for scientific claim extraction by strategically selecting the most informative sentences for human experts to label.
proposals.method: We will build on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and CORD-19-like corpora. Starting with a small seed set of claims labeled via distant supervision from structured abstracts, we will fine-tune a transformer-based language model (e.g., SciBERT). An uncertainty sampling active learning loop (using metrics like entropy or margin) will iteratively select the most uncertain sentences from a large unlabeled pool for expert annotation. This newly labeled data will be used to retrain the classifier, progressively improving its performance on held-out test sets. The process will be evaluated against a baseline of random sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable, cost-effective methodology for building specialized extractors for scientific knowledge. It will advance the infrastructure for Open Knowledge Network development by reducing the human labeling burden. We will release the resulting model and actively learned dataset to the community via the Semantic Scholar platform.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established, its application to bootstrapping scientific claim extraction from full-text papers within an open science infrastructure is novel. The integration of modern pre-trained language models with an active loop specifically designed for the noisy, domain-specific scientific text represents a significant methodological advance over prior bootstrapping techniques.

proposals.title: Personal Knowledge Fabric: Constructing Open Knowledge Networks from Private Document Collections
proposals.motivation: While large-scale Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) like those built from CORD-19 have transformed scientific discovery, the potential of knowledge graphs for personal information management remains untapped. Individuals are overwhelmed by fragmented data in emails, notes, and documents, lacking the structured, queryable knowledge that powers modern AI. This project addresses a critical gap in the 'open science' paradigm by bringing its benefits to the individual scale, enabling more effective personal knowledge work.
proposals.hypothesis: Open Information Extraction (OIE) techniques, adapted with privacy-preserving methods and fine-tuned on personal data distributions, can automatically construct a coherent, queryable personal knowledge graph from a user's unstructured document corpus that significantly outperforms traditional search for complex information retrieval tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first pre-processes a user's email and document archive (e.g., .pdf, .docx). We will adapt and fine-tune pre-2026 OIE frameworks (e.g., based on BERT or T5) on a synthetic corpus mimicking personal communication to better extract (subject, predicate, object) triples from informal language. Extracted triples will be linked to a lightweight, personal ontology and stored in a local graph database (e.g., Neo4j). A key innovation will be a 'contextual salience' module, using a graph neural network to prioritize entities and relationships based on user interaction history.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, user-centric system for personal knowledge graph construction and a benchmark dataset for evaluating personal OIE. It will extend the principles of open knowledge infrastructure to the private domain, creating a new research direction at the intersection of personal informatics, NLP, and human-computer interaction.
proposals.novelty: The application of OKN/OIE methodology to the private, small-data, and linguistically informal domain of personal documents is a significant departure from their typical use on large, formal corpora like scientific publications. The integration of interaction-based salience scoring to manage graph scale and relevance is a novel adaptation for the personal context.

proposals.title: Streaming Open Information Extraction for Real-Time Knowledge Graph Population from News
proposals.motivation: The velocity of information generation, especially in news and scientific reporting (e.g., during the COVID-19 pandemic), has outstripped the ability of static knowledge graphs to stay current. Existing Open Information Extraction (OIE) systems are designed for batch processing of static corpora, creating a significant latency between event occurrence and knowledge base update. This gap hinders real-time applications in areas like public health monitoring, financial analysis, and misinformation tracking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming OIE architecture, built upon incremental graph update algorithms and a drift-aware language model, can extract structured facts from continuous text streams with high precision and low latency, enabling the real-time population of an open knowledge network.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core OIE pipeline from the Semantic Scholar platform for a streaming context. The method involves: 1) A buffered sentence streamer feeding into a transformer-based OIE model (e.g., a fine-tuned T5 or BERT variant) optimized for single-pass inference. 2) A novel incremental clustering module to resolve coreferences and merge extracted (subject, relation, object) triples into a dynamic graph. 3) A temporal confidence scoring mechanism, inspired by CORD-19 metadata analysis, to weight triples based on source recency and corroboration, allowing for fact assertion and retraction over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, publicly available framework for streaming OIE, benchmark datasets of timestamped news streams with ground-truth fact updates, and empirical analysis of temporal information decay and conflict in open knowledge networks. It directly advances the infrastructure for rapid OKN development championed by Etzioni.
proposals.novelty: While batch OIE is mature, shifting to a real-time, streaming paradigm with mechanisms for temporal fact confidence and graph incrementalization is a significant and unsolved challenge. This moves beyond static snapshot extraction to model a living, evolving knowledge base.

proposals.title: Planet-Scale Open Information Extraction: Architectures and Insights from a Web-Scale Knowledge Graph
proposals.motivation: Current Open Information Extraction (OIE) systems operate on curated, limited-scale corpora, missing the vast, dynamic knowledge embedded in the crawlable web. Scaling OIE to a planetary corpus is critical for building comprehensive, real-time Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) that can power next-generation AI and scientific discovery, addressing grand challenges like pandemic response (as seen with COVID-19 CORD-19) and climate science. This aligns with the vision of Semantic Scholar and the AI 2030 report, which emphasize the need for broad, accessible knowledge infrastructures.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed, fault-tolerant architecture leveraging modern graph processing frameworks can successfully execute OIE pipelines on a petabyte-scale web crawl, and the resulting knowledge graph will reveal qualitatively different and more timely factual patterns than those derived from static, scholarly corpora alone.
proposals.method: We will design a cloud-native system using the Apache Spark or Flink ecosystem for distributed processing. The pipeline will integrate a high-recall, neural OIE model (e.g., based on pre-2026 T5 or BART architectures) for initial extraction, followed by a clustering and fusion stage to merge redundant triples across web domains. Storage will utilize a partitioned graph database (e.g., Neo4j or JanusGraph) to manage the extracted (subject, relation, object) triples and their provenance. The system will be validated on the Common Crawl dataset, processing incremental monthly archives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a publicly available, scalable software framework for web-scale OIE, extending the Infrastructure for Rapid OKN Development; 2) a massive, timestamped knowledge graph snapshot derived from the open web, released via the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform; and 3) an analysis of the temporal and geographical biases and novel knowledge frontiers captured at this scale.
proposals.novelty: While distributed text processing is well-studied, applying it to the complex, multi-stage OIE task at a true planetary web scale, with a focus on building a publicly queryable knowledge graph, is an unsolved systems-AI integration challenge. This moves beyond processing a single static corpus (like CORD-19) to a continuous, web-wide knowledge distillation pipeline.

proposals.title: DUAL: Detecting Unreliable Assertions via Language Models for Web Corpus Sanitization
proposals.motivation: The web is a primary corpus for training large language models and constructing open knowledge networks, but it is rife with inaccuracies, misinformation, and low-quality content. Pre-2026, methods for corpus cleaning rely heavily on superficial heuristics like link structure or simple text statistics, which fail to assess factual integrity. This project addresses the critical need for scalable, content-aware quality assessment to improve the reliability of downstream AI systems and scientific knowledge bases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high density of factual assertions that contradict a trusted knowledge source (e.g., a high-quality scientific corpus) is a strong, generalizable indicator of a web page's overall factual unreliability and low quality.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) as a trusted, high-quality scientific knowledge source. Using a pre-trained language model (e.g., T5 or a fine-tuned BERT variant), we will extract factual claims (subject-predicate-object triples) from both the trusted corpus and a large-scale web crawl. We will construct a contradiction graph where nodes are claims and edges represent high-confidence contradictions identified via natural language inference (NLI) models. Web pages will be scored based on the proportion of their extracted claims that participate in contradictions with the trusted source.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable, open-source pipeline for identifying low-quality web content based on factual consistency, directly improving the quality of datasets for AI training. It will contribute a novel, empirically validated metric for web page reliability that moves beyond traditional signals. The resulting flagged corpus and contradiction graph will be released as a public resource for the open science community.
proposals.novelty: While contradiction detection and corpus filtering exist separately, the novelty lies in systematically using extractions from a high-quality scientific corpus as a 'clean reference' to proactively identify and score unreliable web pages at scale, creating a feedback loop where knowledge extraction directly improves corpus quality.

proposals.title: Interpretable Multi-View Consensus Clustering for Scientific Literature with Disagreement-Aware Resolution
proposals.motivation: Scientific knowledge graphs, like those built from the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), integrate heterogeneous views such as text, citation links, and author networks. Current multi-view clustering methods often perform early fusion, forcing a single consensus that can obscure meaningful, view-specific signals. This limits the ability to identify papers that are, for example, topically similar but belong to distinct scholarly communities, a nuance critical for robust literature analysis and discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: A late-fusion consensus clustering framework that explicitly models and interprets inter-view disagreements will produce more robust, stable clusters and surface diagnostically valuable insights that are lost in forced-consensus models.
proposals.method: We will implement independent, optimized clustering models for each metadata view (e.g., BERTopic for text, Louvain for citation graphs, sequence models for temporal interaction data). A pairwise similarity-based consensus mechanism, inspired by techniques like Cluster-based Similarity Partitioning Algorithm (CSPA), will identify a stable 'core' cluster set. For documents with high disagreement, we will employ a diagnostic module that uses interpretable rule generation (e.g., via decision trees on view-specific features) or natural language templates to label the divergence (e.g., 'semantically aligned but citation-isolated').
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel clustering framework that increases robustness by leveraging agreement and enriches analysis by explaining disagreement. It will advance the development of Open Knowledge Networks by providing a method to integrate and reason over multi-faceted scholarly data. The output will be a more nuanced map of scientific literature, directly supporting tasks like literature review and research gap identification.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit treatment of inter-view disagreement not as noise to be overcome, but as a source of interpretable diagnostic information, moving beyond consensus-seeking to consensus-and-diagnosis.

proposals.title: Open Knowledge Synthesis: Context-Aware Clustering via Multi-Relational Graph Embeddings on Scientific Corpora
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), has created a knowledge fragmentation problem. Traditional document clustering methods, based on textual similarity, fail to capture the complex semantic relationships (e.g., causal chains, n-ary interactions) and contextual signals (e.g., citation graphs, author networks) that define true scientific coherence. This impedes the synthesis of knowledge across papers and the rapid development of actionable Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a unified multi-relational knowledge graph from scientific text and its associated metadata, and then performing clustering on its embeddings, will yield more semantically coherent and contextually relevant clusters than text-only or simple entity-graph approaches.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-relational graph from a corpus like CORD-19, where nodes are entities/concepts and edges are diverse relations (e.g., 'inhibits', 'co-occurs-with', 'cited-by') extracted using state-of-the-art language models (e.g., SciBERT). This graph will be augmented with contextual edges from metadata (citation links, author affiliations, URL/domain patterns). We will then generate unified node embeddings using a graph neural network (GNN) architecture like Relational Graph Convolutional Networks (R-GCNs) or CompGCN, designed for multi-relational data. Clustering (e.g., using Leiden or HDBSCAN) will be performed directly on these embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable methodology for synthesizing open scientific knowledge into coherent clusters, directly advancing the infrastructure for OKN development. It will provide a new benchmark for semantic clustering on scientific corpora and release an open framework for integrating complex linguistic constructs with network context, extending platforms like Semantic Scholar.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic fusion of *extracted complex linguistic relations* (beyond simple entity links) with *rich non-textual contextual signals* into a single, learnable graph model for clustering, moving beyond document-level or entity-only analysis.

proposals.title: SHOC-IMP: Sparse Hierarchical Overlapping Clustering with Interpretable Membership Priors for Open Scientific Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of open scientific corpora, such as the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), reveals a critical limitation in current document clustering methods. Real-world scientific articles are inherently multi-faceted, belonging to multiple overlapping themes (e.g., a paper on AI for drug discovery). Standard flat or hierarchical clustering fails to capture this, while unconstrained overlapping models produce degenerate, uninterpretable clusters, hindering downstream tasks like literature search and recommendation within platforms like Semantic Scholar.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a probabilistic model combining a sparse prior on document-cluster membership with a hierarchical non-parametric prior on cluster structure will yield stable, interpretable, and semantically meaningful overlapping clusters that better reflect the true organization of scientific knowledge.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian non-parametric model building on the Hierarchical Dirichlet Process (HDP). A spike-and-slab or Indian Buffet Process (IBP) prior will enforce sparsity in document-to-cluster assignments. The cluster parameters themselves will be generated from a tree-structured prior (e.g., a Nested Chinese Restaurant Process) to share global topics while maintaining distinct sub-topic identities. Inference will be performed via scalable variational or stochastic gradient MCMC, leveraging graph-based representations from existing Open Knowledge Network infrastructure for initialization and evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tractable clustering algorithm specifically designed for the complex, overlapping structure of scientific literature. It will provide the research community with a tool for building more nuanced knowledge graphs from open corpora, directly enhancing tasks like literature discovery and trend analysis. The resulting model and code will be released as part of the Semantic Scholar open platform.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the joint integration of sparsity and hierarchical priors within a single overlapping clustering framework. This moves beyond prior work which typically addresses only one aspect (e.g., overlapping *or* hierarchical structure) and prevents the common degenerate solution of excessive, uninterpretable overlap.

proposals.title: Causal Topic Networks: A Framework for Hypothesis-Driven Synthesis from Open Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: The explosion of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 CORD-19 corpus, has overwhelmed traditional information retrieval. Current systems find relevant documents but fail to synthesize them into testable, causal explanations. This gap hinders scientific discovery, especially in rapidly evolving fields where identifying plausible causal relationships from disparate evidence is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: A probabilistic, overlapping topic network derived from a large corpus can serve as a structured latent layer for causal inference algorithms, enabling the automated generation and ranking of plausible causal hypotheses directly from the text.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and Open Knowledge Network (OKN) infrastructure. First, we will use a neural topic model (e.g., BERTopic) to create a graph of fuzzy, overlapping thematic clusters from a target corpus (e.g., biomedical literature). We will then apply causal structure learning algorithms (e.g., PC, FCI, or NOTEARS) to this network, treating topic co-occurrence and strength as variables to infer potential directed edges. The system will output ranked causal graphs with supporting evidence snippets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable framework for moving from document retrieval to explanatory synthesis. It will provide a method for generating causal hypotheses from unstructured text at web-scale, directly supporting the mission of open science. The resulting tool will be integrated as a new analytical layer for open knowledge networks.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of probabilistic, overlapping topic modeling (for multifaceted document representation) with formal causal inference algorithms, moving beyond correlation to hypothesize causation from the corpus structure itself, rather than relying on pre-defined ontologies or manual curation.

proposals.title: Interactive Exploration of Billion-Tuple Knowledge Graphs via Sub-Linear Approximation and Progressive Summarization
proposals.motivation: The growth of open scientific knowledge graphs, such as those derived from the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) and the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, has created a critical bottleneck. While these massive tuple graphs encode invaluable relationships, traditional linear-time algorithms for centrality and community detection are too slow for interactive user exploration, hindering real-time scientific sensemaking. This problem directly impedes the vision of rapid Open Knowledge Network development and the utility of AI for accelerating science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for user-facing analytics on massive knowledge graphs, interactive exploration and sensemaking can be achieved using sub-linear or constant-time approximation algorithms that generate progressively refined summaries of key entities and relational structures, without requiring exact, linear-time computations.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that integrates graph sampling (e.g., via random walks or forest fire sampling) with online clustering algorithms (e.g., streaming variants of k-means or hierarchical clustering) applied to node embeddings generated by lightweight, incremental language models (e.g., based on BERT or RoBERTa architectures). The core innovation is a progressive summarization engine that, upon a user query, iteratively samples the local graph neighborhood, updates entity and cluster summaries in real-time, and presents a confidence-bounded approximation of centrality and community structure, refining the result as computation continues.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel algorithmic framework for sub-linear summarization of tuple graphs, 2) an open-source interactive exploration system validated on real-world scientific graphs like those from Semantic Scholar, and 3) empirical evidence that human-aligned approximate results are sufficient for exploratory tasks, shifting the paradigm from exact computation to interactive approximation in large-scale knowledge graph analytics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally challenging the necessity of exact computation for user-facing graph analytics and in the integrated design of progressive, sampling-based algorithms that provide theoretically faster, interactive summaries tailored for human sensemaking, rather than batch processing.

proposals.title: Topological Clustering of Scientific Documents for Vocabulary-Agnostic Knowledge Discovery
proposals.motivation: Current document clustering relies on lexical overlap or dense vector similarity, which fails to group documents sharing conceptual structures but using different terminologies. This is a critical bottleneck for open science and knowledge synthesis, particularly in rapidly evolving fields like COVID-19 research, where findings are reported with diverse language. A method to cluster by semantic shape rather than surface features would dramatically improve literature review, hypothesis generation, and the construction of comprehensive Open Knowledge Networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Documents can be effectively clustered by the persistent topological features of their semantic point clouds, enabling the discovery of thematically related scientific literature that exhibits low lexical or standard embedding similarity.
proposals.method: We will represent documents as point clouds using sentence embeddings from pre-2026 models like Sentence-BERT. For each document, we will compute a Vietoris-Rips filtration on its point cloud and extract persistence diagrams using libraries like GUDHI or Ripser. Stable topological features (e.g., connected components, loops) will be vectorized via persistence images or landscapes. These topological descriptors will then be used as inputs for clustering algorithms (e.g., HDBSCAN) and evaluated against ground-truth thematic clusters from datasets like the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19).
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, geometry-driven paradigm for document similarity that is robust to vocabulary shift. It will provide a new tool for the Semantic Scholar platform to uncover latent connections across the scientific literature. The resulting methodology will be a contribution to the infrastructure for developing more nuanced and resilient Open Knowledge Networks.
proposals.novelty: While Topological Data Analysis (TDA) is established in computational biology for shape-based protein analysis, its application to NLP for vocabulary-agnostic semantic clustering is novel. This approach moves beyond the statistical patterns captured by language models to the invariant geometric-topological structure of meaning.

proposals.title: Robustness Under Scrutiny: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Clustering Algorithms Across Dimensions of Web Corpus Diversity
proposals.motivation: Clustering is a foundational tool for organizing the vast, heterogeneous data of the open web, powering applications from scientific literature discovery (e.g., Semantic Scholar, CORD-19) to Open Knowledge Network construction. However, current evaluations typically report aggregate metrics, masking systematic failures on specific data characteristics. This lack of diagnostic understanding hinders the development of robust, generalizable methods for open science and knowledge extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of state-of-the-art clustering algorithms, including Suffix Tree Clustering (STC) and modern neural methods, degrades in predictable ways when confronted with specific, explicit dimensions of corpus diversity (e.g., high noise, narrative style), revealing brittle algorithmic assumptions.
proposals.method: We will construct a large, multi-dimensional web corpus by sampling and explicitly tagging data slices from sources like Common Crawl along axes of formality, domain specificity, narrative style, and noise level. We will evaluate clustering algorithms, including STC and graph-based methods, on this corpus, performing detailed failure analysis per slice. This diagnostic evaluation will correlate performance drops with specific corpus features to identify failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a public diagnostic benchmark for clustering robustness, 2) a set of empirically-grounded principles for algorithm selection and improvement based on corpus characteristics, and 3) actionable insights for building more reliable knowledge extraction pipelines for open web data.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond aggregate performance, this work introduces a controlled, slice-wise diagnostic evaluation framework to explicitly link algorithmic failure to specific dimensions of web data diversity, a granular approach not prevalent in pre-2026 clustering literature.

proposals.title: Generating Contrastive, Query-Grounded Labels for Search Result Clusters Using a T5-Based Sequence-to-Sequence Model
proposals.motivation: Modern web search engines often cluster results to organize diverse information, but the generated cluster labels are typically generic and fail to explain the group's relevance to the specific user query. This hinders rapid comprehension and decision-making, especially for complex or ambiguous information needs. Grounding labels explicitly in the query context is a critical, unsolved problem in information retrieval and human-computer interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequence-to-sequence model trained on query-cluster-snippet triplets can generate cluster labels that are both descriptive and explicitly contrastive, thereby improving a user's ability to understand the thematic grouping in the context of their original search intent.
proposals.method: We will construct a dataset from the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) and general web search logs, creating triplets of (query, set of retrieved snippet texts, human-annotated contrastive label). We will fine-tune a T5 model (pre-trained on C4) to generate the label conditioned on the concatenated query and cluster snippets. The model will be trained with a standard cross-entropy loss, and we will evaluate using ROUGE, BERTScore, and human assessments of label clarity and contrastiveness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method and benchmark dataset for query-contextualized cluster labeling. It will advance the field of interpretable search by producing labels that explicitly link thematic content to user intent, a key step towards more transparent and efficient knowledge discovery systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on generic summarization or static clustering, our method directly conditions label generation on the user's query to produce intent-aware, contrastive explanations. This shifts the focus from describing *what* is in a cluster to explaining *why* it is distinct and relevant to the search goal.

proposals.title: PhyloLabels: A Phylogenetic Nomenclature System for Interpretable Hierarchical Clustering of Scientific Text
proposals.motivation: Hierarchical clustering of documents or web snippets is a core task in organizing scientific knowledge, but current methods produce opaque cluster structures. Labels like 'Cluster 1.2.3' or single-word tags fail to convey the semantic relationships and defining features at each level of the hierarchy, hindering interpretability and navigation. This is a critical barrier for Open Knowledge Networks and tools like Semantic Scholar, where users need to quickly understand the thematic landscape of search results or literature collections.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the principles of phylogenetic nomenclature—where groups are labeled by their shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies)—to text, we can automatically generate a hierarchy of concise, descriptive labels that make the content and relational structure of any nested cluster immediately interpretable to a human.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first performs hierarchical clustering (e.g., using HDBSCAN or agglomerative methods) on document embeddings from models like SPECTER or Sentence-BERT. For each cluster and sub-cluster, the system will identify distinctive 'synapomorphies' by contrasting term and entity frequencies (e.g., via TF-IDF or mutual information) against its sibling and parent clusters. A label synthesis module, using constrained natural language generation or template filling, will combine the top-ranked distinctive features (e.g., key entities, salient n-grams) into a readable, hierarchical label string (e.g., 'Virology > RNA Viruses > Coronaviridae > Betacoronavirus > SARS-CoV-2 > Spike Protein Mutations').
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated labeling system that transforms hierarchical clusters from black-box structures into self-explaining taxonomies. It will directly enhance the interpretability of clustered results in scientific search engines and literature exploration tools. The method and evaluation framework will provide a new benchmark for assessing the utility, not just the accuracy, of document organization systems.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical clustering and keyphrase extraction are well-studied, the explicit analogy to biological systematics for structured label generation is novel. The method's core innovation is its context-aware identification of defining features relative to a cluster's position in the hierarchy, moving beyond flat, context-independent labeling.

proposals.title: Co-Regularized Graph-Topic Fusion for Robust Scientific Document Clustering in Open Knowledge Networks
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 CORD-19 dataset, necessitates robust methods for automatic thematic organization. Existing approaches, such as graph-based community detection on citation networks or topic modeling on text, are brittle: graphs can be sparse or noisy, while bag-of-words models ignore valuable relational context. This fragility hinders the construction of reliable, interpretable Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) from large-scale corpora like Semantic Scholar.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly optimizing a graph community detection objective and a probabilistic topic modeling objective, linked by a co-regularization term, will produce document groupings that are more robust, semantically coherent, and stable than those from either method alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified optimization framework. The graph component will apply spectral clustering or a scalable graph neural network (e.g., GraphSAGE) to a document similarity graph built from citations and textual embeddings. The topic component will use a neural variational inference model for topic modeling. A co-regularization term, measuring the divergence between the cluster assignment distribution from the graph and the topic proportion distribution per document, will be added to the joint loss function, enforced via a penalty like KL-divergence. The model will be trained end-to-end using stochastic gradient descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, robust clustering algorithm specifically designed for large-scale scientific corpora. It will advance the methodology for building OKNs by providing more stable and interpretable document groupings. The resulting system will be validated on the CORD-19 dataset and integrated as a core analysis tool within the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform.
proposals.novelty: While multi-view clustering exists, the tight co-regularization of a relational graph structure with a generative topic model for scientific documents is novel. This approach moves beyond simple ensemble methods to a principled joint optimization where each view actively corrects and informs the other.

proposals.title: Human-in-the-Loop Optimization of Scientific Document Clusters via Interactive Feedback
proposals.motivation: Automated document clustering, while foundational to platforms like Semantic Scholar, often prioritizes mathematical coherence over human utility. This creates a gap between algorithmic output and user needs, hindering effective knowledge discovery. Bridging this gap is critical for advancing open science by building information retrieval systems that genuinely accelerate research.
proposals.hypothesis: That user interaction data from structured search tasks can be used as a feedback signal to iteratively optimize a document network's parameters, resulting in community-detected clusters that measurably improve user performance and satisfaction compared to statically generated clusters.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous document graph from the CORD-19 dataset, using content embeddings (e.g., SPECTER) and citation links. Community detection (e.g., Leiden algorithm) will generate initial clusters. In a controlled user study, participants will perform search tasks; their interactions (clicks, dwell time, task success) will be logged. This behavioral data will train a reinforcement learning agent to adjust graph edge weights and community detection resolution parameters, creating an adaptive, human-optimized clustering model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel methodology for integrating human behavioral signals into the optimization of knowledge networks. It will produce a publicly available, user-tested framework for adaptive document clustering and contribute empirical findings on the alignment between algorithmic structures and human information-seeking behavior.
proposals.novelty: While community detection and user studies exist separately, fusing them into a closed-loop, interactive optimization system for document networks is novel. The approach moves beyond static evaluation metrics (e.g., modularity) to directly optimize for human-centric outcomes.

proposals.title: Open Knowledge Graph Communities: Uncovering Topical Structure in Scientific Literature via Network Science
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 pandemic's publication surge, creates an information overload that hinders discovery and synthesis. Existing search and recommendation systems often fail to reveal the latent, interdisciplinary topical structures that emerge organically across large corpora. This project addresses a core challenge in open science: automatically mapping the intellectual landscape to facilitate navigation and accelerate research.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced community detection algorithms to a heterogeneous document-knowledge graph, built from open scholarly data, will reveal coherent, semantically meaningful topical communities that transcend traditional journal or keyword-based categorizations.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale, heterogeneous graph using the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and CORD-19 dataset. Nodes will represent documents, authors, entities, and keyphrases, with edges based on citations, authorship, and entity co-occurrence. We will employ and compare scalable, attributed graph community detection methods, such as the Leiden algorithm for modularity optimization and potentially Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based approaches like GraphSAGE for node embedding followed by clustering. The resulting communities will be evaluated against established subject classifications and via expert human assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source pipeline for dynamic topic mapping in scholarly networks, directly extending the Infrastructure for Rapid Open Knowledge Network Development. It will provide empirical insights into the convergence and divergence of scientific fields, offering a new tool for literature exploration and meta-science analysis.
proposals.novelty: While community detection is well-studied, its application to a richly attributed, open scholarly graph of this scale—integrating text, citations, and entities—to produce interpretable, field-agnostic topical maps is novel. This moves beyond simple citation clustering to a multi-relational model of scientific knowledge.

proposals.title: PhyloClust: Refining Hierarchical Text Clustering with Phylogenetic Distance Models
proposals.motivation: Hierarchical clustering of scientific text snippets, such as those in the CORD-19 dataset or the Semantic Scholar corpus, is crucial for organizing knowledge and revealing thematic structures. Current methods rely on static, context-agnostic similarity metrics (e.g., cosine on TF-IDF or BERT embeddings) that fail to model the dynamic, lineage-like relationships between evolving concepts. This limits the accuracy and interpretability of the resulting knowledge hierarchies, hindering open science initiatives that depend on robust, automated knowledge organization.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting phylogenetic tree construction models to represent conceptual 'evolution' between text snippets, we can develop a distance metric for hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC) that produces more semantically coherent and accurate topic hierarchies than standard lexical or embedding-based measures.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel distance metric by treating text snippets as 'taxa' and their semantic representations as 'sequences'. Using a graph of snippets linked by citation or temporal proximity (from CORD/S2ORC), we will train a model inspired by the Kimura-2-parameter model to estimate 'conceptual divergence' distances, accounting for semantic drift and multiple 'mutations' (word/concept changes). This distance matrix will then drive a standard HAC algorithm (e.g., Ward's method). We will validate against manually curated hierarchies from scientific review articles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired similarity metric for text clustering that explicitly models conceptual lineage. It will provide a new method for building more accurate and interpretable Open Knowledge Networks from scientific text. The resulting software and models will be released as open-source tools integrated with the Semantic Scholar platform.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods are standard in biology, their application to model conceptual evolution and semantic drift for text clustering is novel. This moves beyond treating similarity as a static geometric property to modeling it as a dynamic, tree-like process of descent.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Multi-Objective Information Bottleneck for Semantic Snippet Compression in Open Knowledge Networks
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 CORD-19 dataset, necessitates automated tools for semantic compression to power efficient retrieval and summarization in platforms like Semantic Scholar. Current Information Bottleneck (IB) methods for text compression optimize for a single, general reconstruction or prediction task, potentially discarding information critical for specific, high-value downstream applications such as identifying key findings or moderating misleading claims. A task-agnostic compression bottleneck can degrade the utility of open knowledge networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the Information Bottleneck framework with a multi-objective, task-aware optimization will yield compressed semantic representations that maintain a Pareto-optimal balance between general efficiency and performance on specified downstream tasks, resulting in more robust and directly useful clusters for targeted applications.
proposals.method: We will adapt the variational Information Bottleneck for text, using a transformer-based encoder to produce a stochastic bottleneck representation. The core IB loss for compressing input snippets and predicting a general label will be jointly optimized with auxiliary losses from lightweight, task-specific diagnostic classifiers (e.g., for relevance to a specific scientific claim or content type). We will employ a multi-task learning framework with adaptive weighting and utilize evolutionary or gradient-based methods to identify the Pareto frontier of optimal trade-offs between compression and the suite of task performances.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tunable compression framework for semantic snippets that is explicitly aligned with downstream use cases, enhancing the efficiency and task-specific accuracy of open knowledge infrastructures. It will provide both a theoretical extension to the IB principle and a practical tool for developers of scientific search and moderation systems to generate semantically robust compressed representations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of multiple, explicit task objectives directly into the Information Bottleneck optimization for text, moving beyond single-task or reconstruction-only compression to produce a Pareto-optimal set of representations tailored for a known ecosystem of applications.

proposals.title: Trait-Based Information Bottleneck: An Ecological Analogy for Interpretable Text Snippet Clustering
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 CORD-19 dataset, creates an information overload where key insights are buried in unstructured text snippets. Current clustering methods applied to dense embeddings from language models like BERT or GPT produce opaque groupings, hindering interpretability and trust in automated knowledge discovery systems. There is a critical need for methods that yield concise, semantically transparent clusters to accelerate open science and knowledge synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: By first mapping text snippets to a structured set of discrete, interpretable functional traits (e.g., communicative intent, entity focus) and then applying the Information Bottleneck principle to this representation, we can produce compressed clusterings that are more interpretable and preserve more predictive information for downstream tasks than clustering raw embeddings directly.
proposals.method: We will define a taxonomy of discrete functional traits for text snippets, such as intent (e.g., claim, method, result), primary entity type, and sentiment polarity, and use a fine-tuned transformer model to annotate snippets from corpora like Semantic Scholar Open Data or CORD-19. We will then formulate a Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) objective where the input is the multi-dimensional trait vector, not raw text. The bottleneck will learn a compressed, discrete clustering that maximizes mutual information with a target variable (e.g., paper domain, citation impact) while minimizing information about the original high-dimensional trait set. Optimization will use a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for discrete cluster assignments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable clustering framework for scientific text that bridges ecological theory and machine learning. It will provide a method to build more transparent components for Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) and scientific search engines. The resulting trait-based clusters will serve as human-analyzable functional groups for literature review and meta-analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the ecological analogy—treating text snippets as species assemblages and applying trait-based functional grouping prior to information-theoretic compression—and in applying the Information Bottleneck to a structured, discrete trait space rather than continuous embeddings, which enforces interpretability by design.

proposals.title: Co-Creative Categorization: A Hybrid STC Algorithm for Personal Knowledge Organization
proposals.motivation: Personal digital collections, such as emails and research notes, are growing rapidly but lack effective organization tools. Purely automated clustering (e.g., STC) often fails to capture user context, while manual filing is burdensome. This project addresses the critical need for systems that respect both algorithmic pattern detection and the user's personal mental model, a gap identified in prior work on Open Knowledge Networks and personal information management.
proposals.hypothesis: A variant of the STC (Suffix Tree Clustering) algorithm, augmented to incorporate user-defined seed categories and interactive feedback, will produce document organizations that are more useful and satisfying than purely automated methods for personal collections.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where users first define a small set of initial, pragmatic categories (e.g., 'Project X emails', 'Grant deadlines'). These categories will be used to bias the STC algorithm, implemented on a graph of document embeddings (e.g., from BERT), by seeding clusters and adjusting distance metrics. The system will present a mixed-initiative interface, allowing users to merge, split, or rename algorithmically suggested clusters, with this feedback directly tuning the underlying model for iterative refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, human-in-the-loop algorithm for personal knowledge organization, bridging Library Science principles with modern NLP. It will also provide an open-source framework and a benchmark dataset of annotated personal collections to advance research in interactive AI for information management.
proposals.novelty: While STC and interactive clustering exist, adapting the pragmatic, hybrid classification systems from personal library science to create a co-creative, biasable STC variant for personal digital collections is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond treating the user as a passive recipient or a mere labeler to an active co-creator of the taxonomy.

proposals.title: Constructing a Coherent Global Opinion Graph: Cross-Lingual Clustering and Belief Propagation for Unified Sentiment Analysis
proposals.motivation: The rapid, multilingual spread of information and misinformation, exemplified during the COVID-19 pandemic, creates fragmented and often contradictory opinion landscapes. Existing sentiment analysis tools are largely monolingual or produce siloed results, failing to provide a consolidated, global view. This impedes researchers, policymakers, and platforms from understanding true consensus and divergence in public discourse across linguistic and cultural boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a heterogeneous, multilingual opinion graph and applying belief propagation algorithms, we can reconcile conflicting sentiment signals across languages to form robust, language-independent opinion clusters, thereby producing a more accurate and coherent model of global public sentiment on a given topic.
proposals.method: We will leverage the CORD-19 and other multilingual web corpora to extract opinion phrases and entities. Using pre-2026 multilingual sentence embeddings (e.g., LaBSE) and co-occurrence statistics, we will construct a graph with nodes representing these textual units and edges weighted by semantic similarity and sentiment polarity. We will then adapt Loopy Belief Propagation (LBP) algorithms to propagate sentiment 'beliefs' across this graph, enforcing global consistency constraints. The stabilized graph will be clustered using scalable community detection methods to identify unified opinion groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel open framework for cross-lingual opinion fusion, directly extending the vision of Open Knowledge Networks. It will provide a new methodology for synthesizing fragmented web-scale sentiment data into a coherent knowledge structure, advancing the field of computational social science and AI for global understanding.
proposals.novelty: While multilingual clustering and belief propagation exist separately, their fusion for opinion reconciliation is novel. The key innovation is the formulation of cross-lingual sentiment consistency as a graph inference problem, moving beyond simple aggregation to a principled, model-based resolution of conflicting signals.

proposals.title: Robust Summarization for Imperfect Clusters: Multi-Faceted Snippet Generation via Intent and Coherence Modeling
proposals.motivation: Real-world document clusters, such as those generated from scientific literature (e.g., COVID-19 CORD-19 corpus) or web search results, are often noisy and semantically diverse. Presenting a single, monolithic summary can mislead users by obscuring key sub-topics or conflicting viewpoints. This problem undermines the utility of large-scale Open Knowledge Networks and semantic search platforms, which rely on accurate, truthful representations of aggregated information for end-users.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling cluster coherence, identifying latent sub-topics, and inferring potential user intents, a system can generate a structured set of complementary snippet candidates that more robustly and truthfully represent the content of a heterogeneous document cluster than a single, consensus-driven summary.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform as a document source. The method involves: 1) constructing a heterogeneous graph of documents, entities, and citations; 2) using graph neural networks and topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic) to assess cluster coherence and identify salient sub-topics and outliers; 3) training a lightweight intent classifier on search log data to model common user goals; and 4) employing a multi-task transformer architecture (building on T5 or BART) conditioned on sub-topic and intent signals to generate a diverse set of candidate snippets with associated meta-information.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for generating transparent, multi-perspective summaries for imperfectly clustered documents, directly enhancing tools for scientific discovery and open knowledge navigation. It will also provide a formalized evaluation methodology for assessing the robustness and truthfulness of cluster representations in information retrieval systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior snippet generation work that assumes cluster homogeneity, this approach explicitly designs for and reveals semantic diversity, treating imperfection as a first-class input. It integrates graph-based coherence analysis with conditional generation to produce a structured summary portfolio, not a single output.

proposals.title: Inverse Summarization: Scaling Seismic Waveform Inversion for Optimal Snippet Generation from Document Clusters
proposals.motivation: Search engines and knowledge platforms like Semantic Scholar rely on high-quality, representative snippets to help users quickly assess document relevance. Current extractive or abstractive methods often fail to capture the core themes and diversity of large document clusters, leading to suboptimal information scent. This problem is magnified in scientific domains like COVID-19 research, where rapidly understanding a corpus's central claims is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing snippet generation as an inverse problem analogous to seismic waveform inversion, we can synthesize optimal representative text by iteratively refining candidate snippets to minimize a loss function defined by cluster representation, diversity, and utility.
proposals.method: We will treat a pre-clustered corpus (e.g., from the CORD-19 dataset) as the latent 'Earth model.' Using a transformer-based language model as a forward model, we will generate synthetic snippet embeddings. An inversion loop will optimize these embeddings via gradient descent to minimize a multi-term loss: (1) centroid similarity (via graph embeddings from an Open Knowledge Network), (2) coverage of cluster diversity, and (3) a learned utility predictor for SERP performance. The final embedding will be decoded into natural language.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable framework for generating high-utility summaries from massive text clusters, directly advancing open science by improving knowledge discovery. It will also contribute a new methodology for applying geophysical inverse problem solvers to high-dimensional NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct, rigorous adaptation of seismic inversion—a method for inferring latent structure from wave data—to the textual domain for generative summarization, moving beyond probabilistic or heuristic approaches dominant in pre-2026 NLP.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Evidence Snippets: A Multi-Modal Inverse Approach to Scientific Figure and Table Clusters
proposals.motivation: Current scientific search engines rely on text snippets, which often fail to convey the core data, methods, or findings embedded in non-textual elements like figures and tables. This creates a 'data blind spot' for users, forcing them to open multiple papers to compare results visually. Leveraging the scale of open corpora like the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) and the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, we can address this by directly surfacing synthesized evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: Clusters of semantically similar non-textual elements (e.g., result figures, methodology tables) from a scientific corpus can be used to generate optimal, multi-modal representative snippets that accurately summarize the shared evidence or methodology across a document cluster.
proposals.method: We will build on the Open Knowledge Network infrastructure to construct a graph linking documents to their extracted figures, tables, and captions. Using pre-2026 multimodal embeddings (e.g., CLIP for figures, structured table representations) and graph clustering algorithms, we will identify clusters of similar non-textual elements across papers. For each cluster, we will treat the generation of a representative snippet as an inverse problem, employing a pipeline that fuses visual summarization (e.g., selecting or synthesizing a canonical figure) with a natural language generator (e.g., a fine-tuned T5 model) conditioned on clustered captions and metadata to produce a concise descriptive summary.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for generating multi-modal search previews that provide a direct view of scientific evidence, moving beyond textual surrogates. It will also deliver a public dataset of clustered scientific figures/tables with generated snippets, advancing research in scientific information retrieval and multi-modal representation learning.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard text-first search paradigm by starting from clusters of non-textual evidence and synthesizing a summary, rather than extracting text from a single document. The application of an inverse problem framework to multi-modal scientific element clusters for snippet generation is unexplored.

proposals.title: Open Intent Graphs: Stable Query Representation Learning for Proactive Knowledge Discovery
proposals.motivation: Modern search and scientific discovery platforms are reactive, responding only after a query is issued. This is inefficient and fails to surface underserved information needs, especially as document corpora like CORD-19 rapidly evolve. A system that can proactively understand and cluster user intent, independent of the volatile document set, would enable more stable and anticipatory knowledge access, a core goal of Open Science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural model trained on historical user interaction signals can learn a dense, intent-aware representation of queries that is stable over time, and that clustering these representations will reveal persistent, actionable user need patterns even as the underlying document corpus changes.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform to construct a large-scale temporal graph of query-document interactions (clicks, dwell time). Using a transformer-based dual-encoder architecture, we will project queries and documents into a shared embedding space optimized via contrastive loss. The learned query embeddings will be clustered using scalable graph-based methods (e.g., Leiden algorithm) on a k-nearest neighbor graph of embeddings. Stability will be evaluated by tracking cluster consistency across time-sliced snapshots of the CORD-19 corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly released model for intent-aware query representation that decouples intent from corpus volatility, 2) a methodology for building stable 'Open Intent Graphs' as a component of Open Knowledge Networks, and 3) demonstrated applications in proactive query recommendation and identifying gaps in scientific information coverage.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior query representation work tied to contemporaneous search results, our approach explicitly models intent as a latent, stable variable, enabling proactive clustering of information needs before a query is made, which is a paradigm shift from reactive search.

proposals.title: Functional Query Clustering via Result-Set Similarity: A Guilt-by-Association Approach for Search Intent Discovery
proposals.motivation: In open science and information retrieval, understanding user intent from ambiguous and terse query text remains a core challenge. Traditional lexical clustering methods fail to capture the functional equivalence of queries that are semantically different but retrieve the same documents. This limits the development of robust intent-aware systems for search personalization and result diversification, which are critical for navigating large-scale open knowledge networks like those built on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clustering web search queries based on the similarity of their top retrieved document sets—analogous to the 'guilt-by-association' principle in protein function annotation—will reveal latent, functionally coherent user intents more accurately than text-based methods.
proposals.method: We will process a large-scale query log (e.g., from the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset or general web search) and issue each query against a standard search index to obtain a fixed-size set of top-k results. We will define a functional similarity metric between queries, such as the Jaccard index over their result-set document IDs. Using this metric, we will construct a query similarity graph and apply scalable graph clustering algorithms (e.g., Louvain community detection) to group functionally equivalent queries. The resulting clusters will be evaluated against manual intent annotations and compared to baseline text-embedding (e.g., BERT) clustering approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operationally grounded method for intent discovery that directly leverages system behavior. It will produce a publicly available dataset of functionally clustered queries, advancing open science in IR. The method will also provide a new primitive for improving query suggestion, session analysis, and ranking models within open knowledge infrastructures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct analogy from computational biology's guilt-by-association principle to query understanding, shifting the focus from query semantics to query function. This bypasses lexical ambiguity by using the retrieval system's response as a proxy for user intent, a perspective underexplored in pre-2026 search research.

proposals.title: Assessing the Sufficiency of a Closed Corpus for Scientific Question Answering: A Controlled Study on the ARC Dataset
proposals.motivation: The AI2 Reasoning Challenge (ARC) Corpus, a large-scale dataset of science exam questions, is a key benchmark for evaluating machine reasoning. A critical, often implicit, assumption in its use is that the provided corpus contains sufficient information to answer its questions, aligning with the goal of testing reasoning over retrieval. If this assumption is false, it confounds the evaluation of reasoning models, as poor performance may stem from incomplete knowledge rather than flawed reasoning. This problem is central to the development of robust, knowledge-grounded AI systems and the principles of open science, where benchmark integrity is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: The ARC Corpus alone is insufficient for correctly answering a significant subset of ARC questions, even for a high-performance retrieval-augmented language model. The missing information constitutes knowledge gaps that are not merely stylistic paraphrases but require external factual or conceptual content.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled experimental framework using the ARC dataset. A state-of-the-art dense passage retriever (e.g., DPR) and reader (e.g., a T5 or BART model) will be trained and evaluated exclusively on the ARC Corpus text. We will then systematically analyze model failures, using human annotators to classify whether the gold-standard answer is definitively supported by text snippets within the corpus. This will be complemented by constructing a knowledge graph from the corpus using OpenIE techniques to explicitly identify missing conceptual nodes and relations required for correct answers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, quantitative assessment of the ARC Corpus's self-contained sufficiency, establishing a clear upper bound for closed-book and retrieval-augmented models on this benchmark. It will produce a curated subset of ARC questions verified to be answerable from the corpus alone, creating a cleaner testbed for reasoning research. The findings will inform future dataset construction for knowledge-intensive QA and the design of Open Knowledge Networks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted the difficulty of ARC questions, no study has systematically isolated and measured the specific contribution of corpus insufficiency versus reasoning complexity to model failure. The approach of combining advanced neural retrieval with structured knowledge graph analysis to audit benchmark completeness is novel.

proposals.title: SciGraphQA: Answering Scientific Questions via Knowledge Graph Completion on the CORD-19 Open Research Graph
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for rapid, precise access to scientific knowledge, as seen in the deluge of literature in the CORD-19 corpus. Traditional search and QA systems often struggle with complex, multi-hop scientific reasoning. Framing QA as link prediction within a structured, open knowledge graph offers a promising path to more accurate and interpretable answers, directly addressing the challenges of open science and accelerating research.
proposals.hypothesis: Scientific Question Answering (QA) can be effectively reframed as a knowledge graph completion task, where answering a question corresponds to predicting a missing link (relation) between entities in a science-specific knowledge graph derived from the corpus.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale, heterogeneous knowledge graph from the CORD-19 dataset and Semantic Scholar data, extracting entities (e.g., genes, diseases, drugs, methods) and relations using pre-2026 NLP tools (e.g., SciSpacy). We will then train and evaluate state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN) and knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models, such as ComplEx, RotatE, and Graph Attention Networks, for the link prediction task. Questions will be parsed into structured queries (head entity, relation type) to predict the missing tail entity as the answer.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, graph-based QA system for scientific literature and a benchmark dataset for evaluating scientific KG completion. It will demonstrate the utility of open knowledge networks (OKNs) for precise information retrieval, advancing both the fields of scientific AI and open knowledge infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While KG completion is well-studied on generic graphs like Freebase, its application to dynamic, complex scientific literature for direct QA, particularly on a corpus as impactful and timely as CORD-19, represents a novel integration of open science, knowledge graphs, and language understanding.

proposals.title: SciWeb: Constructing a Web-Scale Scientific Corpus for Enhanced Knowledge Graph Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The AI2 Reasoning Challenge (ARC) Corpus, while a valuable benchmark for complex question-answering, is limited in scale and diversity, focusing primarily on exam-style questions. To train and evaluate models capable of genuine scientific reasoning, we need a corpus that mirrors the vastness and heterogeneity of real-world scientific discourse. A web-scale corpus would enable the development of more robust language models and knowledge networks, directly advancing open science by providing a foundational resource for the community.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the ARC Corpus with orders-of-magnitude more diverse scientific text, including preprints, patents, textbooks, and project documentation, will significantly improve the performance and generalizability of transformer-based language models and knowledge graph completion algorithms on complex scientific reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will leverage the infrastructure and methodologies from the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and CORD-19 to ingest and process a massive, heterogeneous set of scientific documents. A pipeline will be built using tools like Apache Spark for distributed text extraction and cleaning. We will employ transformer-based models (e.g., BERT, SciBERT) for entity and relation extraction to populate a large-scale Open Knowledge Network (OKN). This network will then be used to generate novel, reasoning-intensive training examples and evaluation sets, extending beyond the ARC format.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the largest publicly available, curated scientific corpus designed for reasoning research, along with an associated knowledge graph. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating scientific understanding in AI and serve as a foundational dataset for accelerating research in open knowledge network development and AI for science.
proposals.novelty: While large text corpora exist, none are specifically engineered and enriched to target the *reasoning* gap in scientific AI. This proposal uniquely combines web-scale data aggregation with structured knowledge extraction and novel task generation, moving beyond passive text collection to active reasoning resource creation.

proposals.title: Inferring Pragmatic Data Schemas via Behavioral Graph Clustering on Query Logs and Structural Metadata
