proposals.motivation: The proliferation of large, heterogeneous, and poorly documented databases—such as those in open science repositories, legacy enterprise systems, or emerging COVID-19 research corpora like CORD-19—poses a significant barrier to knowledge discovery. Traditional exploration requires a formal semantic model (schema or ontology), which is often unavailable, incomplete, or costly to create. There is a pressing need for automated methods to organize and reveal the implicit structure and purpose of data directly from observable usage and relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clustering database entities (tables, columns) based on a fusion of behavioral metadata (query/access patterns) and structural metadata (foreign-key-like links) can accurately infer a 'pragmatic schema' that reflects the data's functional role and latent relationships, even in the absence of explicit semantic labels.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous graph where nodes represent database entities (e.g., columns) and edges encode multiple relationship types: structural links (inferred foreign keys via name similarity and overlap), co-occurrence in queries, and sequential access patterns from query logs. We will apply and compare graph embedding techniques (e.g., Node2Vec, metapath2vec) and multi-view clustering algorithms to group entities into functional clusters. The approach will be validated on open datasets like the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and CORD-19, using manually curated schemas or expert judgment as ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated pipeline for database exploration and schema induction that is agnostic to domain semantics. It will advance the field of Open Knowledge Network development by providing a method to bootstrap network structure from raw data and logs. The resulting techniques will lower the barrier to utilizing complex, unstructured data repositories in scientific and enterprise settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes schema structure or query text separately, this method uniquely fuses behavioral signals (how data is used) with structural metadata to infer a functional, usage-driven organization, moving beyond static schema analysis or pure natural language processing of content.

proposals.title: Expanding the Frontier of Semantic Tractability: A Graph-Language Framework for Complex Scientific Queries
proposals.motivation: Current semantic search and question-answering systems, such as those built on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, excel at factoid queries but struggle with complex, multi-hop, or relationally dense questions common in scientific literature. This limitation hinders the full potential of Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) to accelerate discovery, as seen in the need for rapid synthesis during the COVID-19 pandemic via resources like CORD-19. Broadening the formal class of semantically tractable questions is a critical step toward more powerful AI-assisted science.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating structured knowledge graphs with contextual language models within a formal semantic framework, we can define and efficiently answer a broader class of complex queries—specifically those involving quantification, nested relations, and causal reasoning—that are currently intractable for purely statistical or purely symbolic systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture that leverages the Semantic Scholar knowledge graph as a backbone for explicit relations. A transformer-based language model, fine-tuned on scientific text, will parse complex natural language questions into a formal intermediate representation (e.g., a logical form or a structured query graph). Novel graph neural network (GNN) algorithms will then perform multi-hop reasoning over the knowledge graph, guided by the language model's contextual embeddings to resolve ambiguities and handle implicit relations not directly encoded in the graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formal definition of an expanded class of semantically tractable questions, a publicly available benchmark dataset of complex scientific queries, and an open-source framework for hybrid graph-language reasoning. It will directly advance the infrastructure for rapid OKN development and provide a more robust foundation for next-generation scientific AI assistants.
proposals.novelty: While graph-language models exist, our approach uniquely focuses on formally defining and expanding the *tractability boundary* for semantic querying, moving beyond ad-hoc performance improvements to a principled extension of what questions can be guaranteed to be answered efficiently and reliably from large-scale open scientific corpora.

proposals.title: Modeling the Dynamic Tractability Frontier for Adaptive Query Refinement in Scientific AI Assistants
proposals.motivation: As AI systems for scientific knowledge access, like those built on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, rapidly evolve, their capability boundaries are fluid and opaque to users. This leads to inefficient interactions where users cannot reliably formulate queries that the current system can answer well. An assistant that can adaptively guide query reformulation in sync with the AI's own development is crucial for accelerating open science.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning system can model a dynamic, high-dimensional boundary separating tractable and intractable queries for a given AI model, and that context-aware paraphrases suggested to cross this boundary will significantly improve answer quality and user satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will construct a knowledge graph from CORD-19 and Semantic Scholar data, linking queries, entity contexts, and model performance metrics. Using a graph neural network coupled with a meta-learner (e.g., MAML), the system will learn to predict the 'tractability score' of a query-answer pair for a target model. A controlled text generation module (e.g., based on T5 or GPT-2) will then be trained via reinforcement learning to produce paraphrases that optimize for predicted score increase, using simulated and real user interaction logs as feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel framework for real-time capability assessment in AI systems and a practical tool for adaptive query refinement. It contributes to the science of AI evaluation and human-AI collaboration, specifically within the open knowledge network ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static paraphrase databases or rule-based suggestions, this system explicitly learns a dynamic, model-specific tractability frontier and optimizes paraphrases for navigating it, creating a co-adaptive loop between the AI's core capabilities and its user-facing guidance layer.

proposals.title: Paraphrase-Aware Query Reformulation for Scientific Search via Open Knowledge Graphs and Language Models
proposals.motivation: In open scientific search systems, users often struggle to formulate tractable queries, leading to failed searches and missed information. This is a critical bottleneck in realizing the promise of open science and rapid knowledge discovery, as evidenced by the challenges faced during the COVID-19 pandemic in navigating the CORD-19 corpus. Current systems lack robust mechanisms to diagnose query intractability and suggest actionable, effective paraphrases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid system combining a structured Open Knowledge Network (OKN) with a fine-tuned language model can more accurately identify intractable queries and generate contextually relevant, tractable paraphrases than either approach alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a scientific OKN from Semantic Scholar Open Data, modeling entities and relationships. For a given query, we will use graph metrics (e.g., node degree, path existence) to assess potential tractability. In parallel, we will fine-tune a transformer-based language model (e.g., T5) on query-paraphrase pairs from search logs and synthetically generated data. A decision module will integrate graph-based signals and language model confidence scores to trigger paraphrase suggestion, generating candidate rewrites ranked by predicted retrieval success.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid framework for intelligent query reformulation specifically tailored for scientific search. It will advance the infrastructure for Open Knowledge Networks by demonstrating a concrete application for user interaction. The resulting models and metrics will provide a new benchmark for evaluating query tractability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a large-scale, domain-specific OKN to provide a formal grounding for tractability assessment, moving beyond purely textual or behavioral models of query difficulty. This combines Etzioni's themes of open knowledge infrastructure with state-of-the-art NLP.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Improving Open Knowledge Networks for Robust Natural Language Querying
proposals.motivation: The ability to query structured databases using natural language is a cornerstone of open science, democratizing access to complex data. However, pre-2026 systems are often evaluated on narrow benchmarks (e.g., WikiSQL, Spider), failing to capture the diversity of real-world scientific schemas and the complexity of expert questions. This limits their practical utility for accelerating research across fields like biomedicine, where the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for rapid, intuitive data exploration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current neural semantic parsing and retrieval-augmented language models exhibit significant performance degradation on diverse, domain-specific database schemas and question corpora, and that systematic analysis of failure modes will reveal actionable paths to more robust and generalizable Open Knowledge Network (OKN) interfaces.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel benchmark, SciQuery, by extracting diverse database schemas from public scientific data repositories (e.g., CORD-19, UniProt, ChEMBL) and crowdsourcing/generating associated natural language questions of varying complexity. We will conduct extensive user studies with domain scientists to validate question realism. We will then rigorously evaluate state-of-the-art models (e.g., T5-based semantic parsers, GPT-3/4 with in-context learning, graph-neural-network-enhanced retrievers) on SciQuery, analyzing errors across schema types and question structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A publicly available, challenging benchmark for natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) in scientific domains, 2) A detailed taxonomy of failure modes for current architectures, and 3) Design principles and model enhancements for the next generation of OKN query interfaces, directly advancing the infrastructure for open knowledge discovery.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on general-domain or single-domain benchmarks, our approach systematically stresses-test NL2SQL systems on the extreme schema heterogeneity and specialized jargon of open scientific databases, a critical gap for realizing Etzioni's vision of scalable OKNs.

proposals.title: Semantic Constraint Filtering for High-Precision Knowledge Extraction from Scientific Text
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), has created a pressing need for automated, high-precision knowledge extraction to build Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs). While statistical parsers can generate many candidate relations, they often produce noisy, logically inconsistent outputs that are not tractable for formal reasoning, limiting their utility for downstream scientific applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying formal semantic tractability constraints—such as type consistency, argument structure validity, and logical coherence—as a filter on the output of high-recall statistical parsers will yield a significantly more precise and logically consistent set of extracted relations suitable for integration into structured knowledge graphs.
proposals.method: We will use a high-performance statistical dependency parser (e.g., the AllenNLP parser or a transition-based model) to generate candidate subject-predicate-object triples from scientific abstracts in CORD-19 and Semantic Scholar's Open Corpus. These candidates will then be filtered through a constraint layer defined using a lightweight ontology of scientific entities and relations. Constraints will enforce type signatures (e.g., `inhibits(Virus, Protein)`), arity, and disjointness, implemented via a rule engine or a neural classifier trained on logical inconsistencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, hybrid extraction pipeline that balances recall and precision, directly contributing to the infrastructure for rapid OKN development. It will produce a cleaner, more reliable dataset of scientific relations for domains like biomedicine, advancing the goal of machine-aided scientific discovery.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovates by using formal semantic constraints not as a hard rule for parsing, but as a post-hoc filter on statistical parser output, creating a pragmatic synergy between robust, data-driven NLP and the rigor of symbolic knowledge representation that is under-explored in large-scale scientific text processing.

proposals.title: EXPLAIN-SQL: Generating Natural Language Explanations for Database Query Results Using Knowledge-Enhanced Language Models
proposals.motivation: The democratization of data access through tools like Semantic Scholar's Open Data Platform requires users to interpret complex SQL query results. Pre-2026, research has focused on translating natural language to SQL (NL2SQL), but the inverse problem—explaining SQL results in natural language—remains under-explored. This is critical for open science, enabling researchers, policymakers, and the public to understand data-driven findings without technical expertise, a need highlighted during the COVID-19 pandemic for interpreting scientific data.
proposals.hypothesis: A knowledge-enhanced neural model, trained on a graph of SQL queries, their execution results, and associated scientific text (e.g., from CORD-19), can generate faithful and comprehensible natural language explanations of query results that improve user understanding compared to raw data output.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale training dataset by pairing SQL queries and their result sets from open knowledge graphs (e.g., S2ORC, CORD-19) with relevant explanatory sentences from the source scientific abstracts. The core architecture will be a transformer-based encoder-decoder model. The encoder will process a dual input: a linearized representation of the SQL query's abstract syntax tree (AST) and a structured representation of the key entities/relations in the result set, linked via a pre-existing entity graph (e.g., from the OKN infrastructure). The decoder will generate fluent explanatory text, conditioned on this encoded graph-contextualized input.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dataset and model for the SQL-to-explanation (SQL2NL) task, advancing beyond table-to-text generation by incorporating query intent, 2) a methodology for integrating knowledge graph context into data explanation systems, and 3) a concrete tool for enhancing transparency and interpretability in open scientific data platforms.
proposals.novelty: While table-to-text generation exists, our approach uniquely frames the problem as explaining the *result of a specific query*, not just a static table, and explicitly leverages the structured knowledge graph context from platforms like Semantic Scholar to ground explanations in domain-specific concepts.

proposals.title: Augmenting Scientific Knowledge Graphs with Real-World Evidence: Integrating Clinical Notes and Social Media for a Dynamic Open Knowledge Network
proposals.motivation: The scientific literature, while foundational, is a curated, delayed, and often incomplete record of human knowledge, particularly in fast-moving domains like biomedicine. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical gap between formal publication and real-world evidence, where insights from clinical practice and public discourse on platforms like Twitter (pre-2026) emerged far more rapidly than peer-reviewed papers. This temporal and perspectival lag limits the responsiveness and utility of current knowledge systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating unstructured, real-world data sources—specifically de-identified clinical notes and curated social media discourse—with the structured scholarly corpus will create a more comprehensive, timely, and actionable Open Knowledge Network (OKN) for scientific discovery.
proposals.method: We will extend the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and OKN infrastructure. First, we will apply named entity recognition and relation extraction models (e.g., SciBERT, BioBERT) to de-identified clinical notes from sources like MIMIC-III and social media data filtered for scientific discourse. Second, we will construct a heterogeneous knowledge graph where nodes represent entities (e.g., drugs, symptoms, diseases) from papers, notes, and posts, with edges labeled by source type and confidence. Third, we will use graph neural networks (GNNs) to perform link prediction and identify novel, high-confidence relationships not present in the literature alone, validating them against emerging clinical trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, multi-source OKN prototype for biomedicine that dynamically integrates formal and informal evidence, 2) a methodological framework for assessing the evidential value and temporal lead of non-traditional sources, and 3) concrete case studies (e.g., drug side-effect discovery) demonstrating the network's utility for generating earlier scientific hypotheses.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed clinical notes or social media in isolation, this proposal's novelty lies in the structured, large-scale integration of these streams with the formal literature within a unified, queryable OKN, explicitly challenging the paper as the sole unit of authoritative scientific knowledge.

proposals.title: Beyond Full Text: A Multi-Modal Knowledge Graph for Scientific Discovery from Figures, Tables, and Supplementary Materials
proposals.motivation: Current scientific knowledge extraction, including tools like Semantic Scholar, primarily relies on structured article text, overlooking the dense, curated information in figures, tables, and supplements. This 'non-text' content often contains the most critical findings—experimental results, novel datasets, and methodological details—which are not fully captured by text mining alone. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the urgent need to rapidly synthesize such findings across a vast literature, a process currently bottlenecked by manual extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a multi-modal knowledge graph by jointly mining textual captions, visual figures, and structured tables will surface significant scientific relationships and factual claims that are missed by models trained on full text alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage the CORD-19 and Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform as a corpus. Using a pipeline of pre-2026 models, we will: 1) Extract and classify figures/tables with Detectron2 and rule-based parsers; 2) Employ multimodal transformers (e.g., VL-BERT) to align visual elements with textual captions and referenced passages; 3) Apply structured prediction on table cells using TAPAS; 4) Integrate extracted entities and relations into an Open Knowledge Network (OKN) framework, linking visual evidence to textual claims.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly accessible knowledge graph fusing visual and textual evidence, enabling new query capabilities (e.g., 'show all papers with dose-response curves demonstrating drug X efficacy'). It will provide a benchmark dataset and methodology for evaluating multi-modal scientific information extraction, advancing the infrastructure for rapid knowledge synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work extracts knowledge from text or images separately, this proposal's core novelty is the structured, joint reasoning across modalities within the scientific paper unit to populate a unified knowledge network, directly challenging the assumption that structured full text is sufficient.

proposals.title: From Search to Synthesis: A Knowledge Graph-Augmented LLM Framework for Causal Hypothesis Generation from Scientific Literature
proposals.motivation: Current scientific information retrieval systems, including those built on the CORD-19 dataset, primarily return relevant documents or facts, placing the burden of synthesis and hypothesis generation on the researcher. This is a critical bottleneck in accelerating discovery, especially in fast-moving fields like biomedicine. The field needs systems that move beyond retrieval to actively propose plausible, evidence-based causal relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid system combining a large-scale scientific knowledge graph (like those built on Semantic Scholar data) with a reasoning-capable large language model (LLM) can generate novel, testable causal hypotheses that are more specific and scientifically plausible than those generated by retrieval-only systems or LLMs alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a domain-specific knowledge graph (KG) from the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform, focusing on biomedical entities and relations (e.g., genes, diseases, drugs). This KG will be integrated with a pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT-3 or T5) using a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) architecture. The model will be fine-tuned on structured causal claims from existing literature. Given a user query (e.g., 'potential treatments for long COVID'), the system will retrieve relevant subgraphs from the KG, reason over the paths and connections, and generate ranked causal hypotheses (e.g., 'Drug X may inhibit pathway Y, which is implicated in long COVID symptom Z, based on analogous mechanisms in diseases A and B').
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source framework for AI-assisted scientific discovery that operationalizes the shift from information retrieval to hypothesis generation. It will provide a concrete evaluation benchmark for assessing the novelty and plausibility of machine-generated scientific hypotheses. The resulting system will directly advance the goals of rapid Open Knowledge Network development for science.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses KGs for fact retrieval or LLMs for text generation, this proposal uniquely combines them into a structured reasoning system explicitly designed for causal, interventional hypothesis generation, moving beyond associative search or summarization.

proposals.title: The Living Literature Graph: Integrating Real-Time Clinical and Preprint Feeds into a Dynamic Knowledge Network
proposals.motivation: The scientific literature, as captured in static paper corpora like the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19), represents a lagging indicator of research progress. Critical, time-sensitive information from ongoing clinical trials and rapidly released preprints is siloed, making it difficult for researchers and public health officials to gain a real-time, synthesized view of emerging evidence. This delay hinders rapid scientific response, as seen during the COVID-19 pandemic.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a static scholarly knowledge graph with structured, real-time data feeds from clinical trial registries (ClinicalTrials.gov) and preprint servers (e.g., arXiv, bioRxiv) will create a more comprehensive and timely evidence map, enabling the detection of emerging research trends and evidence gaps weeks to months faster than from published literature alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and Open Knowledge Network (OKN) infrastructure to ingest and harmonize live feeds. A temporal knowledge graph schema will be designed to represent entities (e.g., interventions, outcomes) and their evolving relationships from trials and preprints. We will employ named entity recognition and relation extraction models (e.g., based on BERT or SciBERT) to structure preprint text, and link these entities to the existing paper corpus via graph embedding techniques (e.g., TransE, ComplEx). A dashboard will visualize the dynamic evidence landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible, dynamic knowledge graph that fuses static literature with live research streams, operationalizing the vision of a 'living' evidence system. It will provide a novel benchmark for real-time scientometrics and deliver a tool for accelerating meta-research and evidence synthesis, particularly in fast-moving fields like biomedicine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has built graphs from static corpora (like CORD-19), this proposal dynamically integrates continuously updating, heterogeneous evidence streams into a unified temporal graph, shifting the paradigm from periodic snapshots to a live, queryable knowledge network.

proposals.title: CORD-19-BENCH: A Framework for Systematic Benchmarking of NLP Models on COVID-19 Scientific Literature
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) has catalyzed a surge of NLP applications for scientific knowledge discovery. However, progress is hindered by a lack of standardized, shared evaluation tasks, leading to ad-hoc, non-comparable results. Establishing rigorous, community-adopted benchmarks is essential to objectively measure advancements, foster reproducibility, and accelerate the translation of NLP research into actionable scientific insights for pandemic response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured framework of diverse, well-defined NLP tasks on the CORD-19 corpus, supported by public leaderboards, will enable systematic measurement of model capabilities, reveal specific strengths and limitations of current methods, and drive more focused and comparable progress in scientific NLP.
proposals.method: We will define a suite of core tasks spanning information retrieval, question answering, relation extraction, and summarization, grounded in real scientific use cases. For each task, we will create high-quality training/validation/test splits from CORD-19 metadata and full text, using expert annotation where needed. We will implement baseline models using state-of-the-art pre-2026 architectures (e.g., BERT-based models, T5, and graph neural networks leveraging the Semantic Scholar knowledge graph). An automated evaluation platform will be built to host public leaderboards, enforcing strict submission protocols to ensure fair comparison.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the NLP and biomedical research communities with a standardized, open evaluation suite for CORD-19, enabling direct model comparison and identifying high-performing approaches for scientific information extraction. It will establish a model for creating rapid-response benchmarks for future scientific crises, extending the principles of open science and knowledge networks to evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While CORD-19 is widely used, this proposal moves beyond providing a static dataset to creating a dynamic, multi-task benchmarking *framework* with structured leaderboards. It uniquely integrates knowledge graph context from Semantic Scholar into task design and baseline models, explicitly linking dataset infrastructure to rigorous evaluation methodology.

proposals.title: A Graph-Enhanced Language Model Framework for Normalizing Biomedical Entities and Relations in COVID-19 Literature
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of COVID-19 scientific literature, as indexed in resources like the CORD-19 dataset, has created a critical need for structured knowledge. However, entity and relationship mentions are highly heterogeneous, with the same drug, gene, or disease referred to by multiple names and abbreviations across papers. This inconsistency severely hampers automated reasoning, meta-analysis, and the construction of reliable Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) for biomedical discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid system combining fine-tuned transformer language models with graph-based constraints from biomedical ontologies will achieve superior accuracy in normalizing entity mentions and their asserted relationships to canonical identifiers compared to purely text-based or purely graph-based methods.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform to access a large corpus of COVID-19 papers. The method involves a two-stage pipeline: 1) A fine-tuned BERT or T5 model will perform joint named entity recognition and linking to candidate identifiers from UMLS and other ontologies. 2) A graph neural network (GNN), operating on a subgraph of known relationships from sources like SemMedDB, will refine these predictions by enforcing consistency between normalized entities and the plausibility of the extracted relationship type (e.g., TREATS, INHIBITS).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, normalized knowledge graph derived from CORD-19, enhancing the utility of the dataset for downstream AI tasks. It will also contribute a novel, reproducible framework for entity and relationship normalization that can be generalized to other scientific domains, advancing the infrastructure for rapid OKN development.
proposals.novelty: While entity linking is well-studied, the joint normalization of *both* entities *and* their interconnecting relationships using a synergistic language-and-graph model represents a significant, non-trivial extension. This approach moves beyond siloed entity disambiguation to create a coherent, relationally consistent knowledge substrate.

proposals.title: SciFact-CORD: A Pipeline for Automated Fact-Checking of Scientific Claims in the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered an unprecedented surge of scientific publications, creating an information overload for researchers and the public. Distinguishing well-supported findings from preliminary or retracted claims in real-time is a critical challenge for scientific integrity and public trust. Adapting automated fact-checking methods from journalism to the scientific domain can accelerate the assessment of scientific assertions, aiding in knowledge synthesis and combating misinformation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational pipeline combining claim detection from scientific text with evidence retrieval and verification against a structured knowledge graph can reliably assess the veracity of key scientific assertions in the rapidly evolving COVID-19 literature.
proposals.method: We will construct a pipeline using the CORD-19 dataset as our primary corpus. First, we will fine-tune a transformer-based language model (e.g., SciBERT) for scientific claim detection and extraction. Extracted claims will be linked to entities in a COVID-19-specific Open Knowledge Network (OKN), built from CORD-19 and trusted sources like clinical trial registries. A graph neural network will then score the consistency of a claim with the aggregated network evidence, and a natural language inference model will assess its support from retrieved abstract sentences. The final output will be a veracity score and supporting evidence snippets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, domain-adapted pipeline for scientific claim verification, 2) a publicly available benchmark dataset of labeled scientific claims from CORD-19 for future research, and 3) a demonstration of how journalistic fact-checking techniques can be generalized to accelerate open science, particularly during public health crises.
proposals.novelty: While fact-checking pipelines exist for news, their direct application to the complex, nuanced, and evidence-based discourse of scientific literature is largely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates claim detection, knowledge graph reasoning, and textual entailment specifically for the scientific domain.

proposals.title: The Seminal Corpus: A Graph-Based Method for Curating High-Impact, Cross-Domain Scientific Literature
proposals.motivation: The exponential growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 pandemic's surge in publications, creates information overload, hindering rapid insight generation. While large-scale platforms like Semantic Scholar and CORD-19 provide broad access, researchers lack efficient tools to identify a foundational, high-quality subset of papers that capture the intellectual core of a field or a cross-disciplinary topic. This bottleneck slows down literature review, meta-analysis, and the synthesis of new research directions.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid curation method combining expert-defined criteria with graph-based citation network analysis can algorithmically identify a small, high-impact corpus that retains the seminal knowledge and conceptual structure of a much larger literature.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that ingests a large corpus (e.g., from Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform) and constructs a heterogeneous knowledge graph linking papers, authors, venues, and concepts. We will define 'seminal' via a multi-factor scoring function incorporating citation metrics (e.g., CiteRank), venue prestige, and author prominence. Graph neural networks (GNNs) will then be used to propagate these signals and identify community structures, enabling the selection of a minimal set of papers that maximally cover key conceptual clusters and high-influence pathways. The method will be validated by comparing the curated subset's coverage against expert-curated lists in established domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a reproducible, scalable methodology for creating focused, high-quality literature datasets, accelerating open science. It will contribute a novel benchmark for literature summarization and a publicly available tool for rapid research landscape analysis, directly supporting the development of agile Open Knowledge Networks.
proposals.novelty: While citation analysis exists, this approach novelly frames the problem as one of graph sparsification for knowledge retention, using GNNs to balance impact metrics with topological coverage to preserve a field's conceptual skeleton, moving beyond simple ranking.

proposals.title: PANDORA: A Pandemic-Agnostic Open Knowledge Network for Emerging Infectious Disease Literature
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated the critical need for rapid, machine-readable access to the global scientific literature to accelerate research. However, the CORD-19 corpus and similar efforts are reactive and pathogen-specific, leaving the research community unprepared for the next outbreak. A proactive, scalable infrastructure that continuously integrates literature on *all* emerging infectious diseases (EIDs) is essential for future pandemic preparedness and real-time scientific insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the graph-based infrastructure and entity-centric models developed for COVID-19 can be generalized into a pandemic-agnostic framework, enabling the continuous, automated construction of a comprehensive, queryable knowledge network from heterogeneous EID literature sources.
proposals.method: We will extend the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and OKN (Open Knowledge Network) infrastructure to create PANDORA. The system will employ a continuous ingestion pipeline from PubMed, preprint servers, and global health databases, using a BERT-based classifier to identify EID-relevant papers. A fine-tuned SciBERT model will perform named entity recognition for pathogens, hosts, genes, and interventions, which will be linked to Wikidata and MeSH. These entities and their relationships (e.g., 'inhibits', 'transmits via') will populate a dynamic knowledge graph using the Graph Neural Network (GNN) frameworks previously applied to CORD-19, enabling scalable reasoning and literature-based discovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open, continuously updated knowledge network spanning the entire EID literature domain, moving beyond single-pathogen corpora. It will provide a foundational public resource for predictive modeling of pathogen emergence, drug repurposing, and meta-science analysis of global research trends, directly supporting the open science mission of accelerating response to future health crises.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., CORD-19) focused on a single pandemic, PANDORA's novelty lies in its pathogen-agnostic design and proactive, continuous integration mechanism, transforming a reactive tool into a permanent, anticipatory asset for global health surveillance and research.

proposals.title: OpenGaps: A Large-Scale Dataset of Unanswered Scientific Questions Extracted from the CORD-19 Corpus
proposals.motivation: The scientific literature primarily documents what is known, not what remains unknown. Identifying research gaps is a manual, time-consuming process, slowing scientific progress. A large-scale, structured dataset of unanswered questions would provide a critical resource for directing research efforts, especially in fast-moving fields like biomedicine, and align with the goals of open science and rapid knowledge network development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of discourse analysis and language model prompting can reliably extract and categorize explicit and implicit unresolved questions from scientific text, and that these extracted gaps form a coherent, structured knowledge graph that reveals meaningful patterns in scientific frontiers.
proposals.method: We will leverage the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset (CORD-19) as a foundational corpus. Using a hybrid approach, we will first train a transformer-based classifier (e.g., SciBERT) to identify sentences containing explicit future work or limitation statements. Second, we will employ a prompt-based method with a large language model (e.g., GPT-3) to infer implicit knowledge gaps from the conclusions of papers. Extracted questions will be structured into a knowledge graph, linking them to relevant entities (e.g., genes, diseases, methods) from the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, publicly available dataset of machine-extracted scientific research gaps, enabling new studies on the evolution of scientific questions. It will provide a concrete tool for funding agencies and researchers to identify high-impact opportunities. The methodology will advance techniques for inverse knowledge extraction from literature.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., on citation graphs) infers importance, this directly extracts the *absence* of knowledge as a first-class object. The integration of explicit signal detection with implicit gap inference via LLMs, grounded in a specific corpus like CORD-19, is a novel synthesis of pre-2026 NLP techniques for a new open science resource.

proposals.title: Beyond the Single Sentence: A Graph-Based Framework for Multi-Span Opinion Extraction in Scientific Discourse
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for opinion mining, trained on datasets like MPQA, often assume opinions are confined to single sentences, limiting their utility for analyzing complex scientific arguments. In domains like COVID-19 research, where claims and their supporting evidence are distributed across paragraphs, this simplification obscures the true structure of scientific discourse and consensus. This gap hinders the development of high-fidelity knowledge networks from open scientific corpora like CORD-19.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scientific opinions can be accurately modeled as connected subgraphs within a document's semantic and discourse graph, where nodes represent propositional units and edges represent rhetorical relations (e.g., support, contrast).
proposals.method: We will construct a document-level graph where nodes are text spans (from clauses to sentences) encoded using pre-trained language models (e.g., SciBERT). Edges will be derived from discourse parsers (e.g., PDTB-style) and semantic similarity. We will then formulate opinion extraction as a subgraph identification problem, using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to classify which connected components represent cohesive opinion units. Training data will be created by annotating multi-span opinions in the CORD-19 corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel dataset and model for multi-span opinion extraction, directly advancing the construction of more nuanced Open Knowledge Networks from scientific text. It will provide a new tool for tracking the evolution of claims and evidence in fast-moving research fields, enhancing platforms like Semantic Scholar.
proposals.novelty: While prior work extracts opinions at the sentence level, our approach explicitly models the cross-sentence rhetorical structure of an opinion as a graph, a novel formulation for this task. It integrates discourse theory with graph-based deep learning for scientific NLP.

proposals.title: Temporal Opinion Dynamics in Scientific Review: A Knowledge Graph and Language Model Approach
proposals.motivation: Scientific reviews, such as peer reviews and literature surveys, are often treated as static, independent artifacts, yet they represent a dynamic conversation that evolves with new evidence and perspectives. This static view obscures how consensus forms, how key features of discussion shift, and whether early reviews unduly influence later ones. Modeling this evolution is crucial for open science, as it can reveal biases, improve meta-science, and help platforms like Semantic Scholar better contextualize knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: The trajectory of opinions and discussed features in a sequence of reviews about a scientific artifact (e.g., a paper or dataset) follows predictable patterns that can be modeled as a temporal knowledge graph, where the arrival of new reviews acts as an intervention that shifts the graph's structure and embedded narratives.
proposals.method: We will construct a temporal knowledge graph from review texts, where nodes represent entities (claims, methods, results) and edges represent semantic relationships. Using the CORD-19 dataset and associated reviews as a testbed, we will employ time-stamped Graph Neural Networks (e.g., TGAT or DyRep) to model node and edge evolution. We will integrate pre-trained language models (e.g., SciBERT) for initial node embeddings and to detect shifts in sentiment and feature salience over time, treating each new review as a graph update event.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for modeling the dynamic, interdependent nature of scientific discourse, moving beyond static analysis. It will contribute a new open dataset of temporal review graphs and algorithms to forecast opinion trajectories, directly supporting the development of rapid, evolving Open Knowledge Networks as envisioned in Etzioni's prior infrastructure work.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes reviews in isolation or aggregates them statically, this proposal dynamically models the *sequence* and *interdependence* of reviews as a temporal graph, explicitly testing how later opinions are conditioned on earlier ones—a non-obvious causal structure in review ecosystems.

proposals.title: Inferring Implicit Product Features from Unstructured Reviews via Knowledge-Enhanced Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current sentiment analysis and review mining systems primarily rely on explicit feature mentions, missing a wealth of information conveyed implicitly. For example, a review stating 'the battery died in two hours' implicitly critiques battery life without naming it. This limitation hinders the completeness of automated knowledge extraction from user-generated content, which is critical for building comprehensive Open Knowledge Networks (OKNs) for products and services.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating structured product knowledge graphs with pre-trained language models, we can train a system to accurately infer and ground implicit feature mentions to canonical product attributes, significantly improving recall in feature-based sentiment analysis.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform's infrastructure to construct a training corpus from product reviews and associated specification knowledge graphs (e.g., from Amazon or BestBuy). Using a transformer-based architecture (e.g., T5 or BART), we will formulate the task as a text-to-text generation problem: given a review sentence, generate the canonical feature name. The model will be trained using a multi-task objective combining explicit mention identification, implicit inference, and knowledge graph link prediction, using graph neural networks to encode the product schema context.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available model and benchmark dataset for implicit feature extraction, advancing the state of fine-grained opinion mining. It will also demonstrate a scalable method for enriching OKNs with latent knowledge extracted from text, directly contributing to the infrastructure for rapid OKN development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies explicit features or uses topic modeling, our approach uniquely frames implicit feature resolution as a knowledge-grounded generation task, explicitly connecting unstructured text to a structured product schema via a joint language-graph model.

proposals.title: Open Feature Discovery: Leveraging Topic Models and Knowledge Graphs for Latent Product Feature Extraction from Consumer Reviews
proposals.motivation: Online product reviews are a vast, unstructured source of consumer knowledge, but manually extracting and categorizing product features is intractable at scale. This limits the utility of review data for building comprehensive product knowledge networks, which are critical for recommendation systems and market analysis. An automated, open methodology for feature discovery would significantly accelerate the creation of structured, machine-readable knowledge from this text.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying topic modeling algorithms to large corpora of product review text will reveal coherent, interpretable latent categories that correspond to meaningful product features and attributes, which can be validated and integrated into an open knowledge network schema.
proposals.method: We will apply Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and its neural variant, Neural Topic Model (NTM), to review text from the Amazon Review Dataset and similar open corpora. The pipeline will include text preprocessing (lemmatization, stopword removal), model training to extract topics, and human-in-the-loop validation for topic interpretability. Discovered feature topics will then be linked as nodes within a lightweight Open Knowledge Network (OKN) framework, using Semantic Scholar's S2ORC infrastructure principles for entity resolution and relation mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, open-source pipeline for automated feature extraction from user-generated text, contributing directly to the field of automated knowledge base construction. It will also demonstrate a practical integration of unsupervised topic modeling with structured knowledge networks, advancing methods for populating OKNs from unstructured data.
proposals.novelty: While topic modeling on reviews is not new, the systematic application and validation for the explicit purpose of populating an open, cross-domain product feature ontology within a public knowledge network framework is an underexplored synthesis of NLP and open science infrastructure.

proposals.title: Belief Propagation for Sentiment Consistency in Scientific Review Graphs
proposals.motivation: The rapid growth of scientific literature, exemplified by the COVID-19 CORD-19 dataset, has created a crisis of credibility and information overload. Reviews, citations, and author rebuttals form a complex network of opinions, but current platforms lack tools to model the propagation and consistency of sentiment across this graph. This impedes the efficient synthesis of scientific consensus and the identification of contentious or unreliable claims.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the elements of scientific discourse (papers, reviews, authors) as a heterogeneous graph and applying belief propagation algorithms can enforce global sentiment consistency, surfacing reliable claims and flagging contradictory opinion clusters more effectively than isolated sentiment analysis.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous knowledge graph from open scholarly data (e.g., Semantic Scholar, peer review texts) with nodes for documents, claims, authors, and reviewers, connected by edges representing citation, review sentiment, authorship, and rebuttal. We will apply Loopy Belief Propagation (LBP) to propagate 'belief' scores (credibility/sentiment) across the graph, using node potentials from initial NLP-based sentiment extraction and edge potentials encoding logical consistency constraints (e.g., a rebuttal likely opposes a critical review). The model will be validated on annotated subsets of the CORD-19 and PeerRead datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel graph-based framework for modeling opinion dynamics in science, extending Open Knowledge Network principles to scholarly communication. It will provide a scalable algorithmic tool for platforms to highlight consensus and controversy, directly supporting the goals of open science and rigorous evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While belief propagation is used in social networks, its application to enforce sentiment consistency in the structured, evidence-rich domain of scientific review graphs is novel. This moves beyond simple citation analysis to model the logical flow of agreement and dispute.

proposals.title: Extracting Scientific Claims from Micro-Text: A Graph-Based Approach for Social Media and Short-Form Content
proposals.motivation: The rapid dissemination of scientific information, particularly during events like the COVID-19 pandemic, increasingly occurs on social media platforms and in short-form digital venues. Existing knowledge extraction systems, such as those built on the Semantic Scholar Open Data Platform and CORD-19, are optimized for long-form scholarly text, creating a gap in our ability to systematically capture, validate, and integrate claims and findings from these critical, high-velocity micro-text sources. This limits the completeness and timeliness of Open Knowledge Networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid graph-language model, trained on short scientific discourse, can accurately extract structured scientific claims (e.g., relationships between concepts, methods, and findings) from micro-text such as tweets and post comments, with performance comparable to extraction from abstracts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the extraction infrastructure from the Semantic Scholar platform for very short text. First, we will create a curated dataset of scientific micro-reviews by sampling relevant tweets and Reddit posts, linking them to seed papers in the S2ORC or CORD-19 corpus. We will then develop a model architecture combining a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT or SciBERT) for contextual encoding with a graph neural network that leverages the structured knowledge from the linked papers to disambiguate entities and infer claim relationships in the short text. Training will use distant supervision from the linked paper abstracts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel dataset and model for scientific claim extraction from micro-text, extending the coverage of automated scientific knowledge bases. It will demonstrate a method for rapidly integrating emergent scientific discourse from public platforms into Open Knowledge Networks, enhancing their real-time utility for researchers and the public.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge extraction from long documents is well-studied, applying it to the noisy, context-poor domain of social media posts for scientific claim mining is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the graph-based distant supervision approach that uses linked long-form papers to ground the semantics of ultra-short texts.

ideas.title: Generating Multi-Modal Snippets from Clusters of Document Non-Textual Elements
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption that structured full text is sufficient by focusing on mining clusters of non-textual elements (figures, tables, supplementary files) from scientific corpora. It then applies an inverse problem approach to these clusters, synthesizing optimal, multi-modal representative snippets for search engine display. The synergy enables the generation of search result previews that visually and descriptively summarize the core data or findings shared across a cluster of documents, which is impossible with text-only snippets. This provides users with a direct, synthesized view of the key evidence or results before accessing any full paper.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Semantic Clustering via Multi-Relational Graph Embeddings
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel clustering methodology that integrates extracted complex relational phrases (e.g., n-ary relations, nested events, causal chains) with rich non-textual metadata (link graphs, URL patterns, user interaction trails). Instead of clustering documents or entities based on textual similarity alone, the system constructs a multi-relational knowledge graph where nodes are entities/concepts and edges are the diverse relations extracted from text, augmented with contextual signals from metadata. Clustering is then performed on the unified embeddings of this graph, allowing semantically coherent groups to emerge from the synergy of *what* is said (complex linguistic constructs) and *how* it is situated and used (the metadata and link context).

ideas.title: Reviews are independent and static
ideas.description: Model the evolution of opinions and features over time as new reviews arrive.
ideas.sourceTitle: Extracting Product Features and Opinions from Reviews

ideas.sourceTitle: Open Information Extraction from the Web

ideas.title: Dual: Query Clustering via Functional Response Similarity, Adapted from Protein Function Annotation
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from computational biology. In proteomics, a common solution for annotating protein function is the 'guilt-by-association' method, where proteins are grouped based on the similarity of their interaction partners rather than their sequence. Adapting this, we propose to cluster user queries not by their lexical content but by the similarity of their functional 'output'—the sets of documents they retrieve from a search index. The core method involves defining a similarity metric (e.g., Jaccard index) over the top-k result sets for a large query log. Queries with highly overlapping result sets are clustered together, revealing latent functional groups of user intent. This approach bypasses the ambiguity of query text and directly groups queries by their operational effect on the retrieval system, enabling applications in intent-based query suggestion, search result diversification, and personalized ranking models.

ideas.title: Multi-Faceted Snippet Generation for Heterogeneous Document Clusters
ideas.description: This system addresses the inherent noise and semantic diversity within real-world document clusters by generating structured, multi-perspective summaries rather than a single representative snippet. It first analyzes cluster coherence, identifies key sub-topics and outliers, and models potential user intents. The output is a set of complementary snippet candidates and meta-information, enabling the search interface to dynamically present the most appropriate summary based on context or to reveal cluster diversity explicitly. This approach ensures robust and truthful representation of imperfect clustering results for end-users.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Trait-Based Clustering to the Information Bottleneck for Snippets
ideas.description: This idea applies the information bottleneck principle to compress snippet information into meaningful clusters by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, complex species assemblages are reduced to manageable functional groups based on shared traits that predict ecosystem function. The analogous solution is trait-based clustering. We adapt this by defining a set of interpretable, discrete 'functional traits' for text snippets (e.g., communicative intent, entity focus, sentiment, syntactic function). The standard information bottleneck method is then applied, not to raw text embeddings, but to this structured trait representation. The goal is to find a compressed clustering of snippets that preserves the maximum information about a target output (such as a downstream task label) while minimizing the information from the original, high-dimensional trait set. This creates clusters that are both concise and semantically meaningful, analogous to ecological functional groups.

ideas.title: Create the inverse: a dataset of unanswered questions
ideas.description: Generate a dataset of research gaps and unresolved questions extracted from the papers.
ideas.sourceTitle: CORD-19: The Covid-19 Open Research Dataset

ideas.title: Evaluating and Optimizing Community-Detected Document Clusters via Interactive User Studies
ideas.description: This research fuses network-based document clustering with interactive user evaluation to create an adaptive information retrieval system. A graph of documents is constructed using content and citation features, with community detection algorithms generating candidate topical clusters. These clusters are then presented to users in a controlled search task study, where their interactions, performance, and satisfaction are measured. The key innovation is using the granular behavioral data from the study—such as which clusters users explore, ignore, or find useful for specific tasks—as a feedback signal to adjust the network's link weights, node attributes, or community detection resolution, thereby evolving the model to produce clusters that are not only mathematically coherent but also demonstrably useful to humans.

ideas.title: Co-Regularized Community-Topic Detection for Robust Document Grouping
ideas.description: This system jointly optimizes a network community detection objective and a probabilistic topic modeling objective, enforcing consistency between document clusters found in the similarity graph and latent topic distributions. A co-regularization term penalizes divergence between the two representations, driving the model toward groupings that are both structurally well-defined in the graph and semantically interpretable in the term space. This approach mitigates the sensitivity to initial graph construction by allowing the semantic model to correct for noisy edges and the graph structure to provide relational context missing from bag-of-words models. The result is a more robust and stable set of topical document groups with clearer semantic coherence.

ideas.title: Sub-Linear Network Summarization for Interactive Tuple Graph Exploration
ideas.description: This research investigates whether fundamental network analysis tasks—like identifying central entities and community structures within massive tuple graphs—can be performed effectively in sub-linear or constant time through approximation. We propose algorithms that sample and cluster the graph's relational structure on-the-fly, generating progressively refined summaries of key entities and relations. This approach challenges the need for exact, linear-time computations for user-facing analytics, aiming to prove that interactive exploration and sensemaking of billion-tuple knowledge graphs are possible with theoretically faster, human-aligned approximate results.

ideas.title: Dynamic Tractability Boundary Modeling for Adaptive Paraphrase Suggestion
ideas.description: This research develops a meta-learning system that dynamically identifies questions near the evolving boundary of an AI's capabilities and suggests context-aware paraphrases. It continuously monitors the AI's own performance metrics and user interaction outcomes to model a shifting tractability frontier, rather than relying on a fixed set of rules or examples. The system then generates and ranks paraphrase suggestions not just for lexical diversity, but for their predicted efficacy in moving the query into a more tractable region for the current AI model. This results in an adaptive assistant that improves its guidance in tandem with improvements to the underlying AI system.

ideas.title: Challenging the single-pass assumption

ideas.title: Cross-Lingual Opinion Graph Clustering via Belief Propagation
ideas.description: This research fuses phrase-based multilingual clustering with graph-based belief propagation to model and reconcile opinions across languages. It constructs a heterogeneous graph where nodes are opinion phrases or entities from multilingual web snippets, connected by cross-lingual semantic similarity and co-occurrence edges, with edges weighted by sentiment polarity. Belief propagation algorithms are then applied across this graph to enforce global sentiment consistency, allowing conflicting opinions in different languages to be resolved and forming robust, language-independent clusters of unified sentiment. The result is a system that not only groups related content across languages but also propagates and refines the underlying opinion signals to produce a coherent, consolidated view of global sentiment on a topic.

ideas.title: Scaling down to personal document collections

ideas.title: Leveraging Phylogenetic Nomenclature for Hierarchical Cluster Labeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from biological systematics, where phylogenetic nomenclature provides concise, structured labels for hierarchical groups based on shared derived traits. The analogous problem of naming nested clusters of organisms is solved with a standardized system that reflects evolutionary relationships at each taxonomic rank. Adapted to web snippets, the idea is to develop a method that generates not just a single label for a cluster, but a full hierarchy of descriptive labels for any nested cluster structure. The system would identify the distinctive, shared features (synapomorphies) that define each sub-cluster within its parent context—such as specific named entities, salient terms, or semantic concepts—and synthesize them into concise, human-readable labels. This results in output where the label itself (e.g., 'Technology > Programming Languages > Python > Libraries > Data Science') immediately conveys the cluster's thematic content and its position within the broader taxonomy, greatly improving interpretability and navigation of clustered search results or document collections.

ideas.title: Semantic-Agnostic Database Exploration via Behavioral and Structural Metadata Clustering
ideas.description: This research direction develops techniques to explore and organize the contents of a database when a formal semantic model is unavailable or incomplete. Instead of relying on predefined schemas or analyzing full record text, the method clusters database entities (like tables, columns, or query results) using metadata such as access patterns, join frequencies, foreign key-like link structures, and co-click data from query logs. This creates a 'behavioral schema' that reveals functional clusters and relationships, effectively reverse-engineering a pragmatic understanding of the data's purpose and connections directly from usage and structure, bypassing the need for explicit semantic annotation.

ideas.title: Use Topological Data Analysis to cluster documents by semantic shape instead of lexical overlap
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that document clustering must rely on shared surface phrases or even proximity in a standard dense vector embedding space. Drawing an analogy from computational biology, where protein function is determined by 3D shape rather than linear amino acid sequence, we adapt the solution of topological data analysis (TDA). In the analogous field, persistent homology is used to extract stable topological features (like connected components and loops) from protein structure data, enabling the classification of functionally similar proteins with divergent sequences. Adapted to NLP, documents would be represented as point clouds derived from contextual embeddings (e.g., from sentence transformers) of their constituent sentences or entities. Applying TDA, we would compute persistent homology on these semantic point clouds to identify clusters based on the invariant topological 'shape' of the document's meaning. This method would group documents that share underlying semantic structures and conceptual relationships, even if they use entirely different vocabularies, moving beyond surface or latent lexical similarity to a geometric-topological understanding of document content.

ideas.title: Task-Aware Multi-Objective Information Bottleneck for Semantic Snippet Compression
ideas.description: This approach adapts the Information Bottleneck method to compress text snippets by introducing a task-aware, multi-objective optimization framework. It jointly minimizes the standard IB loss for general compression and prediction of the input while also minimizing auxiliary losses from lightweight diagnostic classifiers for key downstream tasks, ensuring the compressed clusters retain semantically critical information. The system automatically identifies a Pareto-optimal compression trade-off that balances general efficiency with performance on specified high-value tasks. This results in semantically meaningful clusters that are robust and directly useful for targeted applications like accurate retrieval or content moderation.

ideas.title: Multi-View Consensus Clustering with Interpretable Disagreement Resolution
ideas.description: This approach separately clusters documents using independent, optimized models for each coherent metadata type (text, link structure, user interaction sequences). Instead of early fusion, a consensus mechanism identifies stable core clusters agreed upon by multiple views. Critically, documents where views strongly disagree are not forced into a single cluster; the system analyzes the pattern of disagreement to propose meaningful sub-clusters or generate human-interpretable labels explaining the divergence (e.g., 'semantically similar but from distinct link communities'). This yields a final clustering that is both more robust and diagnostically richer than any single-view or naively fused model.

ideas.title: Generating Representative Snippets via Seismic Waveform Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem solution from geophysical seismic waveform inversion to the task of generating optimal representative snippets from document clusters. In seismology, the structure of the Earth is inferred by generating and iteratively refining synthetic waveforms to match observed data. Analogously, here we treat pre-defined document clusters as the latent structure. The system will synthesize candidate text snippets and iteratively refine them by minimizing a loss function that measures how well the snippet represents the cluster's core themes, diversity, and anticipated utility for a searcher. The solution focuses on scaling the inversion process to handle massive, high-dimensional text clusters, producing concise, informative summaries optimized for search engine results pages (SERPs).

ideas.title: Diagnostic Evaluation of Clustering Robustness Across Explicit Dimensions of Web Corpus Diversity
ideas.description: This research constructs a large, multi-dimensional web corpus where data slices are explicitly tagged along defined axes of diversity, such as formality, domain specificity, narrative vs. factual style, and noise level. Clustering methods, including STC, are evaluated not just on overall performance but through a detailed failure analysis across these slices. This approach identifies which algorithmic assumptions break under specific corpus characteristics, providing actionable insights for improving generalizability. The outcome is a diagnostic benchmark and a set of robustness principles for web-scale clustering.

ideas.description: Create a small, expertly curated dataset of seminal papers for rapid insight generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: CORD-19: The Covid-19 Open Research Dataset

ideas.title: Applying Personal Library Classification Systems to Personal Document Collections
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from personal library organization in Library Science to the problem of applying STC to small, personal document collections. In personal libraries, individuals successfully use hybrid classification systems that mix formal structure with highly contextual, pragmatic categories. The adapted idea is to create a variant of the STC algorithm that incorporates this personal, interactive dimension. The system would allow users to define initial personal categories or bias the clustering process, while STC discovers non-obvious thematic links. The resulting organization would be co-created—reflecting both algorithmic pattern detection and the user's own mental model—and would evolve through interaction, making it far more useful for personal email or file collections than a purely automated approach.

ideas.title: Query-Contextualized, Contrastive Labeling for Search Result Clusters
ideas.description: This research develops methods to generate cluster labels for web snippets that are explicitly grounded in the user's query context. Instead of creating generic descriptive titles, the system produces labels that explain why a particular group of results is distinct and relevant to the specific information need. The approach leverages sequence-to-sequence models trained on query-cluster-snippet triplets to generate intent-aware and contrastive phrases. This results in labels that aid user comprehension and decision-making by directly linking the cluster's thematic content to the original search goal.

ideas.title: Sparse Hierarchical Overlapping Clustering with Interpretable Membership Priors
ideas.description: This research develops a novel probabilistic model for document clustering that allows controlled, interpretable multi-faceted membership. The model employs a sparse prior to limit the number of clusters any single document belongs to, preventing degenerate solutions where everything overlaps with everything. Simultaneously, a hierarchical non-parametric prior structures the clusters themselves, allowing them to share global topics while maintaining distinct identities. This results in a system that captures the genuine multifaceted nature of web documents while producing stable, computationally tractable, and semantically meaningful overlapping clusters suitable for downstream tasks like search and recommendation.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Tree Construction Methods for Hierarchical Clustering Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational biology, specifically phylogenetic tree construction, to refine hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC) for text snippets. In phylogenetics, sophisticated evolutionary distance metrics (e.g., Kimura model) are used to build accurate hierarchical trees from genetic sequences, accounting for complex divergence patterns. The adapted research idea applies this principle by developing advanced, context-aware similarity metrics for HAC that model the conceptual 'evolution' and semantic drift between text snippets. This moves beyond standard lexical or simple semantic measures, aiming to cluster snippets based on inferred conceptual lineages and meaningful thematic descent, thereby producing a more nuanced and accurate hierarchical clustering structure.

ideas.title: Causal Topic Networks for Hypothesis-Driven Information Synthesis
ideas.description: This research develops a novel information synthesis framework where documents are assigned probabilistic, overlapping memberships across a network of thematic clusters. This multifaceted representation is then used not for simple query matching, but as the foundational layer for causal inference algorithms. The system analyzes the co-occurrence and strength of concepts within and across these fuzzy clusters to hypothesize potential cause-effect relationships, generating and ranking plausible causal models from the corpus itself. This moves beyond retrieving relevant documents to actively constructing and evidencing explanatory narratives from multifaceted web-scale data.

ideas.title: Leveraging active learning for bootstrapping

ideas.title: Intent-Aware Query Representation Learning for Stable and Proactive Clustering
ideas.description: This system learns dense vector representations for queries by modeling their underlying user intent, independent of the specific documents returned at query time. It trains a neural model on historical query-document interaction signals (e.g., clicks, dwell time, reformulations) to project queries and documents into a shared semantic space. Queries are then clustered based on their intent embeddings, which remain stable even as the document corpus evolves. This enables proactive applications like query recommendation, intent-based search personalization, and the identification of redundant or underserved information needs before a user submits a query.

ideas.title: Relaxing the independence of extractions

ideas.title: Longitudinal Evaluation of Adaptive Search Result Clustering Using Large-Scale, Real-World User Interaction Data
ideas.description: This research fuses large-scale corpus analysis with longitudinal user studies to develop and evaluate adaptive search interfaces that dynamically cluster web results. It involves deploying clustering algorithms (like STC) trained on massive, diverse web corpora within a live search system that adapts its presentation based on implicit and explicit user feedback. The core longitudinal study tracks real users over extended periods, measuring how changes in the adaptive clustering logic—informed by the large-scale corpus analysis—impact navigation efficiency, satisfaction, and learning. This creates a feedback loop where empirical user data from the field continuously refines the understanding of which clustering strategies generalize best from big corpora to real-world use.

ideas.title: Extending Semantic Tractability via Hierarchical Decomposition Inspired by Protein Folding
ideas.description: This research seed aims to broaden the formal class of semantically tractable questions to handle more complex linguistic structures and queries. Drawing an analogy from computational biology's approach to the protein folding problem, we propose a hierarchical decomposition strategy. The analogous solution involved breaking the intractable global prediction task into identifying stable local fragments (secondary structures) and then assembling them under constraints. Adapted to semantic tractability, the research would first define a library of formally verified 'semantic secondary structures'—core, tractable units of compositional meaning. For any complex query, a decomposition algorithm would map it to these fragments. The core innovation is then a formal 'assembly calculus' that specifies the combinatory rules and constraints (e.g., scope, binding, consistency) under which these fragments can be recombined into a global semantic interpretation while guaranteeing that the resulting complex question remains within a provably tractable class. This moves the boundary of tractability from simple, atomic queries to complex, hierarchically structured ones.

ideas.title: Generate new hard science questions
ideas.description: Create an inverse model that outputs questions given a science knowledge base.
ideas.sourceTitle: Think you have Solved Question Answering? Try ARC, the AI2 Reasoning Challenge

ideas.title: Employ Multiscale Community Detection for Hierarchical Topic Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology and biogeography, where multiscale community detection reveals nested structures in species interaction networks, to the context of document analysis. By modeling documents as a graph and applying hierarchical or multiresolution community detection algorithms (e.g., based on multiscale modularity), we can find a hierarchical organization of topical groups. This allows for the simultaneous discovery of broad thematic categories and their constituent, more specific subtopics, providing a richer and more navigable representation of the document corpus's thematic landscape.

ideas.title: Semantic-Aware Paraphrase Generation via Tractable Parse Filtering
ideas.description: This research integrates a statistical parser with a formal semantic tractability checker to not only filter untractable input questions but to diagnose the specific semantic constructs causing intractability. Using this diagnostic, the system then actively guides a paraphrase generation model to produce alternative questions that avoid the problematic constructs while preserving the original intent. This results in an interactive tool that can suggest multiple, semantically tractable paraphrases in real-time, transforming a static filter into an adaptive dialogue agent for question refinement. The core synergy is using deep semantic constraints as a generative guide, making paraphrase suggestion a principled, explainable process rooted in computational semantics.

ideas.title: Use non-phrase-based document representations
ideas.description: Investigate clustering based on dense vector embeddings or latent topics instead of shared surface phrases.

ideas.title: Scale to multimedia reviews
